The first swapped node is the larger value in the first inversion. The second swapped node is the smaller value in the last inversion. If there's only one inversion, both nodes are adjacent in the traversal.
For O(1) space, look into Morris Traversal. It temporarily modifies the tree to create threads back to ancestors, letting you traverse without a stack or recursion.

title: Convert Binary Search Tree to Sorted Doubly Linked List
description:
Convert a **Binary Search Tree** to a sorted **Circular Doubly-Linked List** in place.
Think of the left and right pointers as synonymous to the predecessor and successor pointers in a doubly-linked list. For a circular doubly linked list, the predecessor of the first element is the last element, and the successor of the last element is the first element.
We want to do the transformation **in place**. After the transformation, the left pointer of the tree node should point to its predecessor, and the right pointer should point to its successor. You should return the pointer to the smallest element of the linked list.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Example 3:**
##### **Example 4:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [0, 2000]`
- `-1000 <= Node.val <= 1000`
- `All the values of the tree are unique`
constraints:
The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [0, 2000]
-1000 <= Node.val <= 1000
All the values of the tree are unique
hints: What traversal of a BST gives you nodes in sorted order? Think about how you'd use that traversal to build the linked list.
During the traversal, keep track of the previously visited node. At each step, link the current node with the previous one using left (prev) and right (next) pointers.
After the traversal completes, you'll have a doubly linked list but it's not circular yet. You need to connect the head (first node visited) and the tail (last node visited) to close the loop.

You can do this with a global/instance variable tracking the previous node, or you can collect nodes into a list first and then rewire pointers. The first approach is O(1) extra space beyond the recursion stack, the second is O(n).

title: Construct Binary Search Tree from Preorder Traversal
description:
Given an array of integers `preorder`, which represents the preorder traversal of a BST (i.e., binary search tree), construct the tree and return its root.
It is guaranteed that there is always possible to find a binary search tree with the given requirements for the given test cases.
A binary search tree is a binary tree where for every node, any descendant of `node.left` has a value strictly less than `node.val`, and any descendant of `node.right` has a value strictly greater than `node.val`.
A preorder traversal of a binary tree displays the value of the node first, then traverses `node.left`, then traverses `node.right`.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= preorder.length <= 100`
- `1 <= preorder[i] <= 1000`
- All the values of `preorder` are unique.
constraints:
`1 <= preorder.length <= 100`
`1 <= preorder[i] <= 1000`
All the values of `preorder` are **unique**.
hints: The first element in a preorder traversal is always the root. How can you figure out which elements belong to the left subtree versus the right subtree?
In a BST, all left subtree values are less than the root. So you can scan the preorder array to find where the left subtree ends and the right subtree begins.
Instead of scanning for the split point each time, think about passing an upper bound to each recursive call. A node is valid for the current subtree only if its value is less than the bound.

Use a global index that advances through the preorder array. At each step, if the current value is within the allowed range (less than the upper bound), create a node and recurse for left and right children with updated bounds.

title: My Calendar I
description:
You are implementing a program to use as your calendar. We can add a new event if adding the event will not cause a **double booking**.
A **double booking** happens when two events have some non-empty intersection (i.e., some moment is common to both events).
The event can be represented as a pair of integers `start` and `end` that represents a booking on the half-open interval `[start, end)`, the range of real numbers `x` such that `start <= x < end`.
Implement the `MyCalendar` class:
- `MyCalendar()` Initializes the calendar object.
- `boolean book(int start, int end)` Returns `true` if the event can be added to the calendar successfully without causing a double booking. Otherwise, return `false` and do not add the event to the calendar.
##### **Example 1:**
**Explanation:**
- `MyCalendar myCalendar = new MyCalendar();`
- `myCalendar.book(10, 20);` // return true, no existing events overlap
- `myCalendar.book(15, 25);` // return false, [15, 25) overlaps with [10, 20)
- `myCalendar.book(20, 30);` // return true, [20, 30) does not overlap with [10, 20) since 20 is not in [10, 20)
##### **Constraints:**
- `0 <= start < end <= 10^9`
- At most `1000` calls will be made to `book`.
constraints:
`0 <= start < end <= 109`
At most `1000` calls will be made to `book`.
hints: Two intervals [s1, e1) and [s2, e2) overlap if and only if s1 < e2 AND s2 < e1. This is the overlap condition every approach relies on.
The simplest approach: store all booked intervals in a list, and for each new booking, check it against every existing interval.
To do better than scanning all intervals, think about keeping the intervals sorted. If they're sorted by start time, you only need to find the neighbors of the new interval to check for overlap.

If you maintain a sorted structure (like a TreeMap or sorted list), you can use binary search to find the interval just before and just after the new one. Only those two neighbors can possibly overlap with it.

title: My Calendar II
description:
You are implementing a program to use as your calendar. We can add a new event if adding the event will not cause a **triple booking**.
A **triple booking** happens when three events have some non-empty intersection (i.e., some moment is common to all the three events).
The event can be represented as a pair of integers `start` and `end` that represents a booking on the half-open interval `[start, end)`, the range of real numbers `x` such that `start <= x < end`.
Implement the `MyCalendarTwo` class:
- `MyCalendarTwo()` Initializes the calendar object.
- `boolean book(int start, int end)` Returns `true` if the event can be successfully added to the calendar without causing a triple booking. Otherwise, return `false` and do not add the event to the calendar.
##### **Example 1:**
**Explanation:**
- `MyCalendarTwo myCalendarTwo = new MyCalendarTwo();`
- `myCalendarTwo.book(10, 20);` // return true, the event can be booked.
- `myCalendarTwo.book(50, 60);` // return true, the event can be booked.
- `myCalendarTwo.book(10, 40);` // return true, the event can be booked. [10, 20) is now double-booked.
- `myCalendarTwo.book(5, 15);` // return false, the event causes a triple booking. [10, 15) would be triple-booked with [10, 20) and [10, 40).
- `myCalendarTwo.book(5, 10);` // return true, the event can be booked. [5, 10) is not in the double-booked region.
- `myCalendarTwo.book(25, 55);` // return true, the event can be booked. [25, 40) is now double-booked, but no triple booking exists.
##### **Constraints:**
- `0 <= start < end <= 10^9`
- At most `1000` calls will be made to `book`.
constraints:
`0 <= start < end <= 109`
At most `1000` calls will be made to `book`.
hints: Think about maintaining two lists: one for all bookings and one for the double-booked regions. When a new event arrives, check it against the double-booked regions first.

If the new event overlaps with any double-booked region, it would create a triple booking, so reject it. Otherwise, compute where the new event overlaps with existing single bookings to update the double-booked regions.
The overlap of two intervals [s1, e1) and [s2, e2) is [max(s1, s2), min(e1, e2)). This overlap exists only when max(s1, s2) < min(e1, e2).

An alternative approach: treat each booking as two events (+1 at start, -1 at end) in a sorted map. After tentatively adding the new booking, walk through the map and check if the running count ever reaches 3. If it does, undo the addition.

title: Stock Price Fluctuation
description:
You are given a stream of records about a particular stock. Each record contains a timestamp and the corresponding price of the stock at that timestamp.
Unfortunately due to the volatile nature of the stock market, the records do not come in order. Even worse, some records may be incorrect. Another record with the same timestamp may appear later in the stream correcting the price of the previous wrong record.
Design an algorithm that:
- **Updates** the price of the stock at a particular timestamp, **correcting** the price from any previous records at the timestamp.
- Finds the **latest price** of the stock based on the current records using the latest timestamp.
- Finds the **maximum price** the stock has been based on the current records.
- Finds the **minimum price** the stock has been based on the current records.
Implement the `StockPrice` class:
- `StockPrice()` Initializes the object with no price records.
- `void update(int timestamp, int price)` Updates the price of the stock at the given `timestamp`.
- `int current()` Returns the **latest price** of the stock.
- `int maximum()` Returns the **maximum price** of the stock.
- `int minimum()` Returns the **minimum price** of the stock.
##### **Example 1:**
**Explanation:**
- `StockPrice stockPrice = new StockPrice();`
- `stockPrice.update(1, 10);` // Timestamps are [1] with prices [10].
- `stockPrice.update(2, 5);` // Timestamps are [1, 2] with prices [10, 5].
- `stockPrice.current();` // return 5, the latest timestamp is 2 with price 5.
- `stockPrice.maximum();` // return 10, the maximum price is 10 at timestamp 1.
- `stockPrice.update(1, 3);` // The previous timestamp 1 had the wrong price, so it is updated to 3.
- `stockPrice.maximum();` // return 5, the maximum price is 5 after the correction.
- `stockPrice.update(4, 2);` // Timestamps are [1, 2, 4] with prices [3, 5, 2].
- `stockPrice.minimum();` // return 2, the minimum price is 2 at timestamp 4.
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= timestamp, price <= 10^9`
- At most `10^5` calls will be made in total to `update`, `current`, `maximum`, and `minimum`.
- `current`, `maximum`, and `minimum` will be called only after `update` has been called at least once.
constraints:
`1 <= timestamp, price <= 109`
At most `105` calls will be made **in total** to `update`, `current`, `maximum`, and `minimum`.
`current`, `maximum`, and `minimum` will be called **only after** `update` has been called **at least once**.
hints: You need a hash map to track timestamp-to-price mappings. But how do you efficiently find the max and min price across all timestamps?
A heap (priority queue) can give you the max or min in O(1). But what happens when a price gets corrected? You can't easily remove an arbitrary element from a heap.

Instead of removing corrected prices from the heap, you can use "lazy deletion." Leave the old entry in the heap and check if it's still valid when you peek at the top. An entry is valid if its price matches the current price for that timestamp in your hash map.

Alternatively, a sorted data structure like a TreeMap (or SortedList) that maps prices to their frequency counts lets you add and remove prices in O(log n) and find the max/min in O(log n). When a timestamp's price is corrected, decrement the old price's count and increment the new one.

title: Implement Trie (Prefix Tree)
description:
A trie (pronounced as "try") or prefix tree is a tree data structure used to efficiently store and retrieve keys in a dataset of strings. There are various applications of this data structure, such as autocomplete and spellchecker.
Implement the `Trie` class:
- `Trie()` Initializes the trie object.
- `void insert(String word)` Inserts the string `word` into the trie.
- `boolean search(String word)` Returns `true` if the string `word` is in the trie (i.e., was inserted before), and `false` otherwise.
- `boolean startsWith(String prefix)` Returns `true` if there is a previously inserted string `word` that has the prefix `prefix`, and `false` otherwise.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= word.length, prefix.length <= 2000`
- `word` and `prefix` consist only of lowercase English letters.
- At most `3 * 10^4` calls in total will be made to `insert`, `search`, and `startsWith`.
constraints:
`1 <= word.length, prefix.length <= 2000`
`word` and `prefix` consist only of lowercase English letters.
At most `3 * 104` calls **in total** will be made to `insert`, `search`, and `startsWith`.
hints: Think of each node in the tree as representing a single character. A path from the root to any node spells out a prefix. How would you represent children of a node given that only lowercase letters are used?
You need to distinguish between "a prefix exists in the trie" and "a complete word exists in the trie." Consider adding a boolean flag to each node that marks whether a complete word ends there.

For all three operations (insert, search, startsWith), you traverse the trie character by character from the root. The only difference is what you check at the end: search checks if the final node is marked as a word end, while startsWith only checks if the path exists.

title: Design Add and Search Words Data Structure
description:
Design a data structure that supports adding new words and finding if a string matches any previously added string.
Implement the `WordDictionary` class:
- `WordDictionary()` Initializes the object.
- `void addWord(word)` Adds `word` to the data structure, it can be matched later.
- `bool search(word)` Returns `true` if there is any string in the data structure that matches `word` or `false` otherwise. `word` may contain dots `'.'` where dots can be matched with any letter.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= word.length <= 25`
- `word` in `addWord` consists of lowercase English letters.
- `word` in `search` consists of `'.'` or lowercase English letters.
- There will be at most `3` dots in `word` for `search` queries.
- At most `10^4` calls will be made to `addWord` and `search`.
constraints:
`1 <= word.length <= 25`
`word` in `addWord` consists of lowercase English letters.
`word` in `search` consist of `'.'` or lowercase English letters.
There will be at most `2` dots in `word` for `search` queries.
At most `104` calls will be made to `addWord` and `search`.
hints: Think about a data structure that supports character-by-character prefix matching. A hash set won't work because of the wildcard dots.
A Trie lets you walk through a word one character at a time. For regular characters, you follow the corresponding child. But what do you do when you encounter a dot?
When you hit a dot in the search pattern, you need to try ALL children of the current Trie node. If any path leads to a complete match, return true. This is a DFS with backtracking.

The constraint says at most 3 dots per search query. This means the branching factor is bounded: at most 26^3 paths to explore. Combined with word length at most 25, this keeps the search fast enough.

title: Search Suggestions System
description:
You are given an array of strings `products` and a string `searchWord`. Design a system that suggests at most three product names from `products` after each character of `searchWord` is typed. Suggested products should have common prefix with `searchWord`. If there are more than three products with a common prefix return the three lexicographically minimum products.
Return a list of lists of the suggested products after each character of `searchWord` is typed.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Example 3:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= products.length <= 1000`
- `1 <= products[i].length <= 3000`
- `1 <= sum of products[i].length <= 2 * 10^4`
- All the strings of `products` are unique.
- `products[i]` consists of lowercase English letters.
- `1 <= searchWord.length <= 1000`
- `searchWord` consists of lowercase English letters.
constraints:
`1 <= products.length <= 1000`
`1 <= products[i].length <= 3000`
`1 <= sum(products[i].length) <= 2 * 104`
All the strings of `products` are **unique**.
`products[i]` consists of lowercase English letters.
`1 <= searchWord.length <= 1000`
`searchWord` consists of lowercase English letters.
hints: Since we need the lexicographically smallest products, what happens if we sort the products first? Where would all products sharing a prefix end up?
In a sorted array, products with the same prefix form a contiguous block. You could use binary search to find where that block starts.
After sorting, use two pointers to track the range of products matching the current prefix. As each character is typed, narrow the range from both ends by eliminating products that don't match.

Alternatively, build a Trie from all products. At each Trie node, store up to 3 lexicographically smallest product names that pass through that node. Then each character typed is one Trie traversal step.

title: Longest Word in Dictionary
description:
Given an array of strings `words` representing an English Dictionary, return the longest word in `words` that can be built one character at a time by other words in `words`.
If there is more than one possible answer, return the longest word with the smallest lexicographical order. If there is no answer, return an empty string.
Note that the word should be built from left to right with each additional character being added to the end of a previous word.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= words.length <= 1000`
- `1 <= words[i].length <= 30`
- `words[i]` consists of lowercase English letters.
constraints:
`1 <= words.length <= 1000`
`1 <= words[i].length <= 30`
`words[i]` consists of lowercase English letters.
hints: A word is "buildable" only if every one of its prefixes is also present in the dictionary. How can you efficiently check whether a prefix exists?

If you sort the words by length first, then by lexicographic order, you can process shorter words before longer ones. That way, when you check a word, all its potential prefixes have already been processed.

A Trie represents prefix relationships directly, with one node per prefix. If you insert all words into a Trie, a word is "buildable" if every prefix node along its path is marked as the end of some word.

Think of buildable words as forming chains. You can use DFS/BFS on the Trie, only following edges where the next node is a complete word. The deepest reachable node gives you the longest buildable word.

title: Replace Words
description:
In English, we have a concept called **root**, which can be followed by some other word to form another longer word. Let us call that longer word a **derivative**. For example, when the root `"help"` is followed by the word `"ful"`, we can form the derivative `"helpful"`.
Given a `dictionary` consisting of many roots and a `sentence` consisting of words separated by spaces, replace all the derivatives in the sentence with the root forming it. If a derivative can be replaced by more than one root, replace it with the root that has the **shortest** length.
Return the `sentence` after the replacement.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= dictionary.length <= 1000`
- `1 <= dictionary[i].length <= 100`
- `dictionary[i]` consists of only lower-case letters.
- `1 <= sentence.length <= 10^6`
- `sentence` consists of only lower-case letters and spaces.
- The number of words in `sentence` is in the range `[1, 1000]`.
- The length of each word in `sentence` is in the range `[1, 1000]`.
- Every two consecutive words in `sentence` will be separated by exactly one space.
- `sentence` does not have leading or trailing spaces.
constraints:
`1 <= dictionary.length <= 1000`
`1 <= dictionary[i].length <= 100`
`dictionary[i]` consists of only lower-case letters.
`1 <= sentence.length <= 106`
`sentence` consists of only lower-case letters and spaces.
The number of words in `sentence` is in the range `[1, 1000]`
The length of each word in `sentence` is in the range `[1, 1000]`
Every two consecutive words in `sentence` will be separated by exactly one space.
`sentence` does not have leading or trailing spaces.
hints: For each word in the sentence, you need to find the shortest root that is a prefix. Think about what data structure lets you check if a string exists in a collection in O(1).
If you store all roots in a set, you can check every prefix of a word (starting from length 1) until you find one in the set. The first match is guaranteed to be the shortest.

For a faster solution, build a Trie from the roots. As you walk character-by-character through a word, the first time you reach a node marked as the end of a root, you have found the shortest matching root.

When building the Trie, you do not need to insert past a node that is already marked as the end of a root. If "ca" and "cat" are both roots, inserting "cat" past the node for "ca" is unnecessary because "ca" will always be chosen first.

test_cases.input: ["cat","bat","rat"], "the cattle was rattled by the battery"
test_cases.expected_output: "the cat was rat by the bat"

test_cases.input: ["a","aa","aaa","aaaa"], "a aa a aaaa aaa aaa aaa aaaaaa bbb baba ababa"
test_cases.expected_output: "a a a a a a a a bbb baba a"

test_cases.input: ["catt","cat","bat","rat"], "the cattle was rattled by the battery"
test_cases.expected_output: "the cat was rat by the bat"

test_cases.input: ["z"], "a b c d e f g"
test_cases.expected_output: "a b c d e f g"

title: Maximum XOR of Two Numbers in an Array
description:
Given an integer array `nums`, return the maximum result of `nums[i] XOR nums[j]`, where `0 <= i <= j < n`.
##### **Example 1:**
**Explanation:** The maximum result is `5 XOR 25 = 28`.
##### **Example 2:**
**Explanation:** The maximum result is `36 XOR 91 = 127`.
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= nums.length <= 2 * 10^4`
- `0 <= nums[i] <= 2^31 - 1`
hints: Think about how XOR works at the bit level. To maximize the result, which bit positions matter most?
Try building the answer one bit at a time, starting from the most significant bit. At each step, can you check whether it's possible to set the next bit to 1?

If you know the prefix of the maximum XOR up to bit k, you can use the property that if a XOR b = c, then a XOR c = b. Store all number prefixes in a set and check whether a desired XOR prefix is achievable.
Alternatively, think about a trie of binary representations. For each number, traverse the trie greedily choosing the opposite bit at every level to maximize XOR.

title: Remove Sub-Folders from the Filesystem
description:
Given a list of folders `folder`, return the folders after removing all sub-folders in those folders. You may return the answer in **any order**.
If a `folder[i]` is located within another `folder[j]`, it is called a sub-folder of it. A sub-folder of `folder[j]` is any folder that starts with `folder[j]` followed by a `"/"`.
The format of a path is one or more concatenated strings of the form: `'/'` followed by one or more lowercase English letters.
For example, `"/leetcode"` and `"/leetcode/problems"` are valid paths while an empty string and `"/"` are not.
##### **Example 1:**
**Explanation:** Folders `"/a/b"` is a subfolder of `"/a"` and `"/c/d/e"` is a subfolder of `"/c/d"`. The remaining folders are not subfolders of any other folder.
##### **Example 2:**
**Explanation:** Folders `"/a/b/c"` and `"/a/b/d"` are subfolders of `"/a"`.
##### **Example 3:**
**Explanation:** No folder is a subfolder of another. Note that `"/a/b/ca"` is not a subfolder of `"/a/b/c"` because the next character after `"c"` is `"a"`, not `"/"`.
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= folder.length <= 4 * 10^4`
- `2 <= folder[i].length <= 100`
- `folder[i]` contains only lowercase English letters and `'/'`.
- `folder[i]` always starts with the character `'/'`.
- Each folder name is unique.
constraints:
`1 <= folder.length <= 4 * 104`
`2 <= folder[i].length <= 100`
`folder[i]` contains only lowercase letters and `'/'`.
`folder[i]` always starts with the character `'/'`.
Each folder name is **unique**.
hints: If you sort the folder list, what property do parent folders have relative to their sub-folders in the sorted order?
After sorting lexicographically, every sub-folder appears immediately after its closest ancestor. You only need to compare each folder with the most recently accepted "root" folder.

To check if folder B is a sub-folder of folder A, verify two things: B starts with A, AND the character in B right after A's length is '/'. Just checking startsWith is not enough because "/a/bc" starts with "/a/b" but is not a sub-folder of "/a/b".

Alternatively, think about building a trie where each node represents a path component (not a character). Mark nodes where a complete folder path ends, then collect only folders whose path hits a marked node for the first time.

title: Find the Length of the Longest Common Prefix
description:
You are given two arrays with **positive** integers `arr1` and `arr2`.
A **prefix** of a positive integer is an integer formed by one or more of its digits, starting from its **leftmost** digit. For example, `123` is a prefix of the integer `12345`, while `234` is **not**.
A **common prefix** of two integers `a` and `b` is an integer `c`, such that `c` is a prefix of both `a` and `b`. For example, `5655359` and `56554` have a common prefix `565` while `1223` and `43456` **do not** have a common prefix.
You need to find the length of the **longest common prefix** between all pairs of integers `(x, y)` such that `x` belongs to `arr1` and `y` belongs to `arr2`.
Return the length of the longest common prefix among all pairs. If no common prefix exists among them, return `0`.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= arr1.length, arr2.length <= 5 * 10^4`
- `1 <= arr1[i], arr2[i] <= 10^8`
constraints:
`1 <= arr1.length, arr2.length <= 5 * 104`
`1 <= arr1[i], arr2[i] <= 108`
hints: Instead of comparing every pair of numbers directly, think about what information you can precompute from one array to speed up lookups from the other.
Each number has at most 9 digits, so it produces at most 9 prefixes, a small bounded amount of work per element. Can you store all prefixes from one array and look them up efficiently?

Build a set of all prefixes from arr1 (for number 12345, store 1, 12, 123, 1234, 12345). Then for each number in arr2, generate its prefixes and check which ones exist in the set. Track the longest match.
You can generate prefixes by repeatedly dividing the number by 10. Starting from the full number and dividing strips off the last digit each time, giving you every prefix from longest to shortest.

title: Words Within Two Edits of Dictionary
description:
You are given two string arrays, `queries` and `dictionary`. All words in each array comprise of lowercase English letters and have the same length.
In one **edit** you can take a word from `queries`, and change any letter in it to any other letter. Find all words from `queries` that, after a **maximum** of two edits, equal some word from `dictionary`.
Return a list of all words from `queries`, that match with some word from `dictionary` after a maximum of **two edits**. Return the words in the **same order** they appear in `queries`.
##### **Example 1:**
**Explanation:**
- Changing the 'r' in "word" to 'o' allows it to equal the dictionary word "wood".
- Changing the 'n' to 'j' and the 't' to 'k' in "note" allows it to equal "joke".
- It would take more than 2 edits for "ants" to equal a dictionary word.
- "wood" can equal itself by making 0 edits.
##### **Example 2:**
**Explanation:** Applying any two edits to "yes" cannot make it equal to "not".
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= queries.length, dictionary.length <= 100`
- `n == queries[i].length == dictionary[j].length`
- `1 <= n <= 100`
- `queries[i]` and `dictionary[j]` consist of lowercase English letters.
constraints:
`1 <= queries.length, dictionary.length <= 100`
`n == queries[i].length == dictionary[j].length`
`1 <= n <= 100`
All `queries[i]` and `dictionary[j]` are composed of lowercase English letters.
hints: Since all strings have the same length, you only need to count character-by-character mismatches. No need for full edit distance.
For each query word, compare it against every dictionary word. Count positions where the characters differ. If the count is 2 or less, the query word is valid.
You can short-circuit the comparison: as soon as the mismatch count exceeds 2, stop comparing and move to the next dictionary word.
For a more efficient approach, consider building a Trie from the dictionary and searching it with a DFS that allows up to 2 mismatches at each level.

title: Word Search II
description:
Given an `m x n` board of characters and a list of strings `words`, return all words on the board.
Each word must be constructed from letters of sequentially adjacent cells, where adjacent cells are horizontally or vertically neighboring. The same letter cell may not be used more than once in a word.
##### **Example 1:**
*The words "eat" and "oath" can both be found on the board by following adjacent cells. "pea" and "rain" cannot be formed.*
##### **Example 2:**
*The word "abcb" cannot be formed because it would require reusing the cell at position (0,1).*
##### **Constraints:**
- `m == board.length`
- `n == board[i].length`
- `1 <= m, n <= 12`
- `board[i][j]` is a lowercase English letter.
- `1 <= words.length <= 3 * 10^4`
- `1 <= words[i].length <= 10`
- `words[i]` consists of lowercase English letters.
- All the strings of `words` are unique.
constraints:
`m == board.length`
`n == board[i].length`
`1 <= m, n <= 12`
`board[i][j]` is a lowercase English letter.
`1 <= words.length <= 3 * 104`
`1 <= words[i].length <= 10`
`words[i]` consists of lowercase English letters.
All the strings of `words` are unique.
hints: Running Word Search I for each word independently is too slow when the word list is large. Think about a data structure that lets you check prefixes of multiple words at the same time.

Insert all words into a Trie. Then, for each cell on the board, start a DFS and walk down the Trie simultaneously. If the current path does not match any prefix in the Trie, prune that branch immediately.
When you find a complete word during DFS, add it to your results. But do not stop the DFS there because longer words might share the same prefix and continue deeper in the Trie.
To avoid duplicates and improve performance, remove a word from the Trie once you find it. You can also prune empty Trie branches to avoid exploring dead-end paths in future searches.

title: Stream of Characters
description:
Design a `StreamChecker` class as follows:
- `StreamChecker(String[] words)` - A constructor that initializes the object with the strings array `words`.
- `boolean query(char letter)` - Accepts a new character from a stream of characters, and returns `true` if any non-empty suffix of the stream of characters so far is equal to some word in `words`.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= words.length <= 2000`
- `1 <= words[i].length <= 200`
- `words[i]` consists of lowercase English letters.
- `letter` is a lowercase English letter.
- At most `4 * 10^4` calls will be made to `query`.
constraints:
`1 <= words.length <= 2000`
`1 <= words[i].length <= 200`
`words[i]` consists of lowercase English letters.
`letter` is a lowercase English letter.
At most `4 * 104` calls will be made to query.
hints: Instead of checking if any word is a suffix of the stream, think about it in reverse. What if you reversed all the words and searched from the end of the stream backward?
A Trie is great for prefix matching. If you reverse all the words and build a Trie, then searching backward through the stream becomes a prefix search in the reversed-word Trie.
You only need to look back at most maxWordLength characters in the stream. So you do not need to store the entire stream history if memory is a concern.

For each query, start from the most recently added character and walk backward through the stream while simultaneously traversing the Trie. Stop as soon as you find a complete word or fall off the Trie.

title: Concatenated Words
description:
Given an array of strings `words` (**without duplicates**), return all the **concatenated words** in the given list of words.
A **concatenated word** is defined as a string that is comprised entirely of at least two shorter words (not necessarily distinct) in the given array.
##### **Example 1:**
**Explanation:**
- "catsdogcats" can be concatenated as "cats" + "dog" + "cats"
- "dogcatsdog" can be concatenated as "dog" + "cats" + "dog"
- "ratcatdogcat" can be concatenated as "rat" + "cat" + "dog" + "cat"
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= words.length <= 10^4`
- `1 <= words[i].length <= 30`
- `words[i]` consists of only lowercase English letters.
- All the strings of `words` are **unique**.
- `0 <= sum(words[i].length) <= 10^5`
constraints:
`1 <= words.length <= 104`
`1 <= words[i].length <= 30`
`words[i]` consists of only lowercase English letters.
All the strings of `words` are **unique**.
`1 <= sum(words[i].length) <= 105`
hints: This problem is closely related to the Word Break problem (LeetCode 139). If you can solve Word Break for a single string, you can solve this by applying it to every word in the array.
Put all words into a HashSet for O(1) lookup. For each word, check if it can be split into two or more parts where each part is in the set. Be careful not to count the word as matching itself.
A DP array where dp[i] means "the first i characters can be formed by concatenating words from the set" works well. For each position i, try every possible last-word ending at i.
Since each word is at most 30 characters long, you don't need to check all O(L^2) substrings. For each position i, you only need to look back at most 30 characters to find a valid word ending at i.

title: Word Squares
description:
Given an array of **unique** strings `words`, return all the **word squares** you can build with them. The same word from `words` can be reused multiple times. You can return the answer in **any order**.
A sequence of strings forms a valid **word square** if the k-th row and k-th column read the exact same string, where `0 <= k < max(numRows, numColumns)`.
For example, the word sequence `["ball","area","lead","lady"]` forms a word square because each word reads the same both horizontally and vertically.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= words.length <= 1000`
- `1 <= words[i].length <= 4`
- All `words[i]` have the same length
- `words[i]` consists of only lowercase English letters
- All `words[i]` are unique
constraints:
1 <= words.length <= 1000
1 <= words[i].length <= 4
All words[i] have the same length.
words[i] consists of only lowercase English letters.
All words[i] are unique.
hints: Think about building the word square row by row. Once you place the first few rows, the symmetry property constrains what the next row must start with.
If you have placed rows 0 through i-1, the required prefix for row i is formed by reading column i from the words you have already placed.
You need a way to quickly find all words that start with a given prefix. A hash map from prefix to list of words works, but a trie gives you a cleaner structure for prefix lookups.
At each step, extract the required prefix from the columns of already-placed words, find all words matching that prefix, and try each one. Backtrack when no words match.

title: Palindrome Pairs
description:
You are given a **0-indexed** array of **unique** strings `words`.
A **palindrome pair** is a pair of integers `(i, j)` such that:
- `0 <= i, j < words.length`,
- `i != j`, and
- `words[i] + words[j]` (the concatenation of the two strings) is a palindrome.
Return an array of all the **palindrome pairs** of `words`.
You must write an algorithm with `O(sum of words[i].length)` runtime complexity.
##### **Example 1:**
The palindromes are `["abcddcba","dcbaabcd","slls","llssssll"]`
##### **Example 2:**
The palindromes are `["battab","tabbat"]`
##### **Example 3:**
The palindromes are `["a","a"]`
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= words.length <= 5000`
- `0 <= words[i].length <= 300`
- `words[i]` consists of lowercase English letters.
constraints:
`1 <= words.length <= 5000`
`0 <= words[i].length <= 300`
`words[i]` consists of lowercase English letters.
hints: If word A is the exact reverse of word B, then A + B is always a palindrome. But that only covers one case. What other combinations can form palindromes?
Think about splitting a word into two parts: a prefix and a suffix. If the prefix is itself a palindrome, what does the suffix need to pair with?

For each word, try every possible split point. If the left part is a palindrome, check if the reverse of the right part exists in the word list. Similarly, if the right part is a palindrome, check if the reverse of the left part exists. A hash map makes these lookups O(1).
Be careful with the empty string case. An empty string paired with any palindromic word produces a valid pair in both directions.

title: Last Stone Weight
description:
You have a collection of stones, each stone has a positive integer weight.
Each turn, you choose the **two heaviest** stones and smash them together. Suppose the stones have weights `x` and `y` with `x <= y`. The result of this smash is:
- If `x == y`, both stones are destroyed.
- If `x != y`, the stone of weight `x` is destroyed, and the stone of weight `y` has new weight `y - x`.
At the end, there is **at most one** stone left. Return the weight of the last remaining stone. If there are no stones left, return `0`.
##### **Example 1:**
**Explanation:** We combine 7 and 8 to get 1, so the array converts to [2, 4, 1, 1, 1], then we combine 2 and 4 to get 2, so the array converts to [2, 1, 1, 1], then we combine 2 and 1 to get 1, so the array converts to [1, 1, 1], then we combine 1 and 1 to get 0, so the array converts to [1], then that's the last stone weight.
##### **Example 2:**
**Explanation:** There is only one stone, so we return its weight directly.
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= stones.length <= 30`
- `1 <= stones[i] <= 1000`
constraints:
`1 <= stones.length <= 30`
`1 <= stones[i] <= 1000`
hints: Each round you need to quickly find the two largest stones. What data structure gives you the maximum element efficiently?
A max-heap (or priority queue) lets you extract the maximum in O(log n) time. After smashing, you can insert the difference back in O(log n) time.
In languages like Python where the heap is a min-heap by default, you can negate all values to simulate a max-heap. Insert -stone and extract -heap[0] to get the maximum.

title: Minimum Cost to Connect Sticks
description:
You have some number of sticks with positive integer lengths. These lengths are given as an array `sticks`, where `sticks[i]` is the length of the `ith` stick.
You can connect any two sticks of lengths `x` and `y` into one stick by paying a cost of `x + y`. You perform this action until there is one stick remaining.
Return the **minimum cost** of connecting all the given sticks into one stick in this way.
##### **Example 1:**
**Explanation:** You start with sticks = [2, 4, 3]. First, combine sticks 2 and 3 for a cost of 5. Now you have sticks = [5, 4]. Then combine sticks 5 and 4 for a cost of 9. Now you have one stick = [9]. The total cost is 5 + 9 = 14.
##### **Example 2:**
**Explanation:** Combine 1 and 3 for cost 4, sticks = [4, 8, 5]. Combine 4 and 5 for cost 9, sticks = [9, 8]. Combine 9 and 8 for cost 17. Total = 4 + 9 + 17 = 30.
##### **Example 3:**
**Explanation:** There is only one stick, so no connections are needed. The total cost is 0.
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= sticks.length <= 10^4`
- `1 <= sticks[i] <= 10^4`
constraints:
1 <= sticks.length <= 10^4
1 <= sticks[i] <= 10^4
hints: Think about which sticks contribute the most to the total cost. Sticks that are merged early get their lengths counted in many future merge operations.
To minimize total cost, you want the longest sticks to be merged as late as possible. That means always merging the two shortest sticks first.
What data structure lets you efficiently find and remove the two smallest elements, then insert a new element back?
A min-heap supports extract-min in O(log n) and insert in O(log n). Build the heap from the input, then repeatedly extract the two smallest, merge them, and insert the result back.

title: Furthest Building You Can Reach
description:
You are given an integer array `heights` representing the heights of buildings, some `bricks`, and some `ladders`.
You start your journey from building `0` and move to the next building by possibly using bricks or ladders.
While moving from building `i` to building `i+1` (0-indexed),
- If the current building's height is **greater than or equal** to the next building's height, you do **not** need a ladder or bricks.
- If the current building's height is **less than** the next building's height, you can either use **one ladder** or `(h[i+1] - h[i])` **bricks**.
Return the furthest building index (0-indexed) you can reach if you use the given ladders and bricks optimally.
##### **Example 1:**
**Input:** `heights = [4,2,7,6,9,14,12]`, `bricks = 5`, `ladders = 1`
**Output:** `4`
##### **Example 2:**
**Input:** `heights = [4,12,2,7,3,18,20,3,19]`, `bricks = 10`, `ladders = 2`
**Output:** `7`
##### **Example 3:**
**Input:** `heights = [14,3,19,3]`, `bricks = 5`, `ladders = 0`
**Output:** `1`
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= heights.length <= 10^5`
- `1 <= heights[i] <= 10^6`
- `0 <= bricks <= 10^9`
- `0 <= ladders <= heights.length`
constraints:
`1 <= heights.length <= 105`
`1 <= heights[i] <= 106`
`0 <= bricks <= 109`
`0 <= ladders <= heights.length`
hints: Think about when to use a ladder vs. bricks. Ladders are most valuable for the biggest climbs since they cover any height difference for the cost of just one ladder.

What if you used ladders first for every climb, and when you run out of ladders, swap the smallest climb back to bricks? A min-heap can help you track the smallest climb currently covered by a ladder.
Maintain a min-heap of size at most ladders. Each time you encounter a climb, push it onto the heap. If the heap size exceeds ladders, pop the smallest climb and pay for it with bricks instead.
If at any point your remaining bricks go negative after paying for a popped climb, you cannot proceed further. Return the current building index.

title: Single-Threaded CPU
description:
You are given `n` tasks labeled from `0` to `n - 1` represented by a 2D integer array `tasks`, where `tasks[i] = [enqueueTime_i, processingTime_i]` means that the `i-th` task will be available to process at `enqueueTime_i` and will take `processingTime_i` to finish processing.
You have a single-threaded CPU that can process **at most one** task at a time and will act in the following way:
- If the CPU is idle and there are no available tasks to process, the CPU remains idle.
- If the CPU is idle and there are available tasks, the CPU will choose the one with the **shortest processing time**. If two tasks have the same processing time, it will choose the task with the **smaller index**.
- Once a task is started, the CPU will **process the entire task** without stopping.
- The CPU can finish a task then start a new one instantly.
Return the order in which the CPU will process the tasks.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `tasks.length == n`
- `1 <= n <= 10^5`
- `1 <= enqueueTime_i, processingTime_i <= 10^9`
constraints:
`tasks.length == n`
`1 <= n <= 105`
`1 <= enqueueTimei, processingTimei <= 109`
hints: Think about how a real CPU scheduler works. At each decision point, the CPU only considers tasks that have already arrived. How can you efficiently track which tasks are available at any given time?
Sort the tasks by their enqueue time so you can easily determine which tasks have arrived by any given time. But remember to keep track of the original indices since the output requires them.

Use a min-heap (priority queue) to hold the available tasks, ordered by processing time (and index as a tiebreaker). When the CPU finishes a task, add all newly available tasks to the heap, then pop the one with the shortest processing time.
Watch out for idle gaps: if the heap is empty but unprocessed tasks remain, the CPU must jump forward in time to the next task's enqueue time.

title: Process Tasks Using Servers
description:
You are given two **0-indexed** integer arrays `servers` and `tasks` of lengths `n` and `m` respectively. `servers[i]` is the **weight** of the `i`th server, and `tasks[j]` is the **time needed** to process the `j`th task **in seconds**.
Tasks are assigned to the servers using a **task queue**. Initially, all servers are free, and the queue is empty.
At second `j`, the `j`th task is inserted into the queue (starting with the `0`th task being inserted at second `0`). As long as there are free servers and the queue is not empty, the task in the **front of the queue** will be assigned to a free server with the **smallest weight**, and in case of a tie, it is assigned to the free server with the **smallest index**. If there are no free servers and the queue is not empty, we wait until a server becomes free and immediately assign the next task. If multiple servers become free at the same time, then multiple tasks from the queue are assigned **in order** as described above.
A server that is assigned task `j` at second `t` will be free again at second `t + tasks[j]`.
Return a 0-indexed integer array `ans` of length `m`, where `ans[j]` is the **index** of the server the `j`th task will be assigned to.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `servers.length == n`
- `tasks.length == m`
- `1 <= n, m <= 2 * 10^5`
- `1 <= servers[i], tasks[j] <= 2 * 10^5`
constraints:
`servers.length == n`
`tasks.length == m`
`1 <= n, m <= 2 * 105`
`1 <= servers[i], tasks[j] <= 2 * 105`
hints: Think about what data structures let you efficiently find the minimum-weight free server and the next server to become free.
Use two priority queues: one for free servers (ordered by weight, then index) and one for busy servers (ordered by the time they become free).
For each task j, first move all servers whose free time is at or before the current time from the busy heap back to the free heap. Then assign the top of the free heap to the task.
If no server is free at the current second, fast-forward time to the earliest moment a server becomes free. Don't simulate second by second.

title: Longest Happy String
description:
A string `s` is called **happy** if it satisfies the following conditions:
- `s` only contains the letters `'a'`, `'b'`, and `'c'`.
- `s` does not contain any of `"aaa"`, `"bbb"`, or `"ccc"` as a substring.
- `s` contains **at most** `a` occurrences of the letter `'a'`.
- `s` contains **at most** `b` occurrences of the letter `'b'`.
- `s` contains **at most** `c` occurrences of the letter `'c'`.
Given three integers `a`, `b`, and `c`, return **the longest possible happy string**. If there are multiple longest happy strings, return any of them. If there is no such string, return the empty string `""`.
##### **Example 1:**
**Input:** `a = 1`, `b = 1`, `c = 7`
**Output:** `"ccaccbcc"`
**Explanation:** `"ccbccacc"` would also be a correct answer.
##### **Example 2:**
**Input:** `a = 7`, `b = 1`, `c = 0`
**Output:** `"aabaa"`
**Explanation:** It is the only correct answer in this case.
##### **Constraints:**
- `0 <= a, b, c <= 100`
- `a + b + c > 0`
hints: Think about which character you should place next. If you always pick the character with the highest remaining count, you use up the dominant character as fast as possible. Why might that be a good strategy?
You cannot always pick the most frequent character. If the last two characters in your string are the same, placing a third would violate the rule. In that case, what should you pick instead?

When the most frequent character would cause three in a row, pick the second most frequent character as a "separator." This breaks the run and lets you go back to the most frequent character on the next step.

A max-heap (priority queue) makes it easy to always know which character has the highest remaining count. At each step, pop the top. If it matches the last two characters of the string, pop the second element instead, use it, and push the first one back.

title: Maximum Performance of a Team
description:
You are given two integers `n` and `k` and two integer arrays `speed` and `efficiency`, both of length `n`. There are `n` engineers numbered from `1` to `n`. `speed[i]` and `efficiency[i]` represent the speed and efficiency of the `i-th` engineer respectively.
Choose at most `k` engineers out of the `n` engineers to form a team with the maximum performance.
The performance of a team is the sum of its engineers' speeds multiplied by the minimum efficiency among its engineers.
That is, `performance = sum(speed) * min(efficiency)`.
Return the maximum performance of this team. Since the answer can be a huge number, return it modulo `10^9 + 7`.
##### **Example 1:**
**Input:** `n = 6`, `speed = [2,10,3,1,5,8]`, `efficiency = [5,4,3,9,7,2]`, `k = 2`
**Output:** `60`
**Explanation:** We select engineer 2 (speed=10, efficiency=4) and engineer 5 (speed=5, efficiency=7): performance = (10 + 5) * min(4, 7) = 60.
##### **Example 2:**
**Input:** `n = 6`, `speed = [2,10,3,1,5,8]`, `efficiency = [5,4,3,9,7,2]`, `k = 3`
**Output:** `68`
**Explanation:** We select engineer 1, engineer 2, and engineer 5: performance = (2 + 10 + 5) * min(5, 4, 7) = 68.
##### **Example 3:**
**Input:** `n = 6`, `speed = [2,10,3,1,5,8]`, `efficiency = [5,4,3,9,7,2]`, `k = 4`
**Output:** `72`
**Explanation:** We select engineers 1, 2, 4, and 5: performance = (2 + 10 + 1 + 5) * min(5, 4, 9, 7) = 72.
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= k <= n <= 10^5`
- `speed.length == n`
- `efficiency.length == n`
- `1 <= efficiency[i] <= 10^8`
- `1 <= speed[i] <= 10^5`
constraints:
`1 <= k <= n <= 105`
`speed.length == n`
`efficiency.length == n`
`1 <= speed[i] <= 105`
`1 <= efficiency[i] <= 108`
hints: What if you knew which engineer has the minimum efficiency in your team? How would you pick the remaining engineers?

If you sort engineers by efficiency in decreasing order and process them one by one, each engineer you process could be the one with the minimum efficiency. All previously processed engineers have higher or equal efficiency, so they're all eligible teammates.
You want the top-k speeds among eligible engineers. A min-heap of size k lets you maintain the k largest speeds seen so far. When the heap exceeds size k, remove the smallest speed.

At each step, the current performance is speedSum * currentEfficiency, where speedSum is the sum of speeds in your heap and currentEfficiency is the efficiency of the engineer you just processed (the current minimum). Track the maximum across all steps.

title: Find Median from Data Stream
description:
The **median** is the middle value in an ordered integer list. If the size of the list is even, there is no middle value, and the median is the mean of the two middle values.
- For example, for `arr = [2, 3, 4]`, the median is `3`.
- For example, for `arr = [2, 3]`, the median is `(2 + 3) / 2 = 2.5`.
Implement the `MedianFinder` class:
- `MedianFinder()` initializes the `MedianFinder` object.
- `void addNum(int num)` adds the integer `num` from the data stream to the data structure.
- `double findMedian()` returns the median of all elements so far. Answers within `10^-5` of the actual answer will be accepted.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `-10^5 <= num <= 10^5`
- There will be at least one element in the data structure before calling `findMedian`.
- At most `5 * 10^4` calls will be made to `addNum` and `findMedian`.
**Follow up:**
- If all integer numbers from the stream are in the range `[0, 100]`, how would you optimize it?
- If `99%` of all integer numbers from the stream are in the range `[0, 100]`, how would you optimize it?
constraints:
`-105 <= num <= 105`
There will be at least one element in the data structure before calling `findMedian`.
At most `5 * 104` calls will be made to `addNum` and `findMedian`.
hints: You don't need the entire collection sorted. You only need quick access to the middle element(s). What data structure gives you fast access to the maximum or minimum?
Think about splitting all numbers into two halves: a lower half and an upper half. The median comes from the boundary between these two halves.

A max-heap can give you the largest element of the lower half in O(1), and a min-heap can give you the smallest element of the upper half in O(1). If you keep both heaps balanced in size, the median is right at their tops.
When adding a number, insert it into the appropriate heap, then rebalance so the heaps differ in size by at most 1. The median is either the top of the larger heap or the average of both tops.

title: IPO
description:
Suppose LeetCode will start its **IPO** soon. In order to sell a good price of its shares to Venture Capital, LeetCode would like to work on some projects to increase its capital before the IPO. Since it has limited resources, it can only finish at most `k` distinct projects before the IPO. Help LeetCode design the best way to maximize its total capital after finishing at most `k` projects.
You are given `n` projects where the `i-th` project has a pure profit `profits[i]` and a minimum capital of `capital[i]` is needed to start it.
Initially, you have `w` capital. When you finish a project, you will obtain its pure profit and the profit will be added to your total capital.
Pick a list of **at most** `k` distinct projects from given projects to **maximize your final capital**, and return the final maximized capital.
The answer is guaranteed to fit in a 32-bit signed integer.
##### **Example 1:**
**Input:** `k = 2`, `w = 0`, `profits = [1,2,3]`, `capital = [0,1,1]`
**Output:** `4`
##### **Example 2:**
**Input:** `k = 3`, `w = 0`, `profits = [1,2,3]`, `capital = [0,1,2]`
**Output:** `6`
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= k <= 10^5`
- `0 <= w <= 10^9`
- `n == profits.length`
- `n == capital.length`
- `1 <= n <= 10^5`
- `0 <= profits[i] <= 10^4`
- `0 <= capital[i] <= 10^9`
constraints:
`1 <= k <= 105`
`0 <= w <= 109`
`n == profits.length`
`n == capital.length`
`1 <= n <= 105`
`0 <= profits[i] <= 104`
`0 <= capital[i] <= 109`
hints: At each step, you want the most profitable project you can afford. Think about what data structure gives you fast access to the maximum element.
If you sort projects by their capital requirement, you can incrementally add projects to a "pool" of affordable options as your capital grows.
Use a max-heap to track the profits of all affordable projects. Each round, push all newly affordable projects into the heap, then pop the maximum profit.
After sorting by capital, use a pointer to track which projects have been added to the heap. Since your capital only increases, you never need to re-check projects already added.

title: Sliding Window Median
description:
The **median** is the middle value in an ordered integer list. If the size of the list is even, there is no middle value, and the median is the mean of the two middle values.
- For example, for `arr = [2, 3, 4]`, the median is `3`.
- For example, for `arr = [2, 3]`, the median is `(2 + 3) / 2 = 2.5`.
You are given an integer array `nums` and an integer `k`. There is a sliding window of size `k` which is moving from the very left of the array to the very right. You can only see the `k` numbers in the window. Each time the sliding window moves one position to the right.
Return the median array for each window in the original array. Answers within `10^-5` of the actual value will be accepted.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= k <= nums.length <= 10^5`
- `-2^31 <= nums[i] <= 2^31 - 1`
hints: When the window slides by one, only one element is added and one is removed. Think about maintaining a sorted structure incrementally rather than re-sorting each time.
To find the median efficiently, you need quick access to the middle element(s). Two heaps (a max-heap for the lower half and a min-heap for the upper half) give you O(1) median access.

The tricky part with heaps is removing the outgoing element. Since heaps don't support efficient arbitrary removal, consider "lazy deletion": mark elements as removed and clean them up only when they appear at the top of a heap.
Alternatively, in languages with sorted containers (Java's TreeMap, C++ multiset), you can insert and remove in O(log k) directly.

title: Kth Largest Element in a Stream
description:
Design a class to find the `k`th largest element in a stream. Note that it is the `k`th largest element in the sorted order, not the `k`th distinct element.
Implement `KthLargest` class:
- `KthLargest(int k, int[] nums)` Initializes the object with the integer `k` and the stream of integers `nums`.
- `int add(int val)` Appends the integer `val` to the stream and returns the element representing the `k`th largest element in the stream.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= k <= 10^4`
- `0 <= nums.length <= 10^4`
- `-10^4 <= nums[i] <= 10^4`
- `-10^4 <= val <= 10^4`
- At most `10^4` calls will be made to `add`.
- It is guaranteed that there will be at least `k` elements in the array when you search for the `k`th element.
constraints:
`0 <= nums.length <= 104`
`1 <= k <= nums.length + 1`
`-104 <= nums[i] <= 104`
`-104 <= val <= 104`
At most `104` calls will be made to `add`.
hints: You don't need to keep track of all elements in sorted order. Think about which elements matter for answering "kth largest."
If you only keep the k largest elements you've seen so far, the smallest among them is your answer. What data structure gives you quick access to the minimum?

A min-heap of size k always keeps the kth largest element at the top. When a new element arrives, only add it if it's larger than the current minimum (the heap's root). Then remove the root to maintain size k.

title: Top K Frequent Elements
description:
Given an integer array `nums` and an integer `k`, return the `k` most frequent elements. You may return the answer in any order.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= nums.length <= 10^5`
- `-10^4 <= nums[i] <= 10^4`
- `k` is in the range `[1, the number of unique elements in the array]`.
- It is guaranteed that the answer is unique.
**Follow up:** Your algorithm's time complexity must be better than O(n log n), where n is the array's size.
constraints:
`1 <= nums.length <= 105`
`-104 <= nums[i] <= 104`
`k` is in the range `[1, the number of unique elements in the array]`.
It is **guaranteed** that the answer is **unique**.
hints: Start by counting the frequency of each element. What data structure gives you O(1) lookups for counting?
Once you have the frequencies, you need the top k. Sorting all entries works, but can you do better? Think about what happens if you only need to track k items at a time.
A min-heap of size k naturally evicts the least frequent element when a more frequent one arrives. But there's an even faster approach: what if the maximum possible frequency is bounded by n?
If you create an array where index i holds all elements with frequency i, you can walk from the highest index downward and collect k elements in O(n) total time.

title: K Closest Points to Origin
description:
Given an array of `points` where `points[i] = [xi, yi]` represents a point on the X-Y plane and an integer `k`, return the `k` closest points to the origin `(0, 0)`.
The distance between two points on the X-Y plane is the Euclidean distance (i.e., `sqrt((x1 - x2)^2 + (y1 - y2)^2)`).
You may return the answer in **any order**. The answer is **guaranteed** to be unique (except for the order that it is in).
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= k <= points.length <= 10^4`
- `-10^4 <= xi, yi <= 10^4`
hints: You do not need the actual Euclidean distance. Comparing x^2 + y^2 is enough since the square root is a monotonically increasing function.
The simplest approach is to sort all points by distance. But do you really need to sort the entire array when you only need the k smallest?

Think about a data structure that can efficiently track the k smallest elements as you scan through the array. A max-heap of size k lets you quickly check if a new point is closer than the farthest point in your current top-k.
For an even faster approach, consider quickselect. After partitioning, all elements to the left of the pivot are smaller. If the pivot lands at index k, you are done. No need to sort either half.

title: Summary Ranges
description:
You are given a sorted unique integer array `nums`.
A range `[a, b]` is the smallest sorted list of every number between `a` and `b` (inclusive).
Return the smallest sorted list of ranges that cover all the numbers in the array exactly. That is, each element of `nums` is covered by exactly one of the ranges, and there is no integer `x` such that `x` is in one of the ranges but not in `nums`.
Each range `[a, b]` in the list should be output as:
- `"a->b"` if `a != b`
- `"a"` if `a == b`
##### **Example 1:**
**Explanation:** The ranges are `[0,2]`, `[4,5]`, and `[7,7]`. Since 7 is a single number, we format it as `"7"` instead of `"7->7"`.
##### **Example 2:**
**Explanation:** The ranges are `[0,0]`, `[2,4]`, `[6,6]`, and `[8,9]`.
##### **Example 3:**
**Explanation:** There are no numbers to cover, so the result is empty.
##### **Constraints:**
- `0 <= nums.length <= 20`
- `-2^31 <= nums[i] <= 2^31 - 1`
- All the values of `nums` are unique.
- `nums` is sorted in ascending order.
constraints:
`0 <= nums.length <= 20`
`-231 <= nums[i] <= 231 - 1`
All the values of `nums` are **unique**.
`nums` is sorted in ascending order.
hints: Since the array is sorted, consecutive numbers will always be adjacent. Think about what condition tells you a range has ended.
Track where each range starts. As you scan, keep moving forward as long as the next element is exactly one more than the current. When that condition breaks, the range is complete.

You can use a two-pointer style approach: one pointer marks the start of the current range, and another scans forward to find the end. When the end is found, format the range and move the start to the next unprocessed element.
For formatting, compare the start and end of each range. If they are equal, output a single number. Otherwise, output "start->end".

title: Meeting Rooms
description:
Given an array of meeting time intervals where `intervals[i] = [start_i, end_i]`, determine if a person could attend all meetings.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `0 <= intervals.length <= 10^4`
- `intervals[i].length == 2`
- `0 <= start_i < end_i <= 10^6`
constraints:
0 <= intervals.length <= 10^4
intervals[i].length == 2
0 <= start_i < end_i <= 10^6
hints: If the meetings were in chronological order, checking for overlaps would be much simpler. You'd only need to look at neighboring meetings.
Sort the meetings by their start time. Now think about what condition makes two consecutive meetings overlap.
After sorting, two consecutive meetings overlap if the current meeting starts before the previous one ends. In other words, if intervals[i][0] < intervals[i-1][1], there's a conflict.

title: Meeting Rooms II
description:
Given an array of meeting time intervals `intervals` where `intervals[i] = [start_i, end_i]`, return the minimum number of conference rooms required.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= intervals.length <= 10^4`
- `0 <= start_i < end_i <= 10^6`
constraints:
1 <= intervals.length <= 10^4
0 <= start_i < end_i <= 10^6
hints: Think about what happens at each point in time. A meeting starting means you need one more room. A meeting ending means a room is freed up.
What if you sorted the meetings by start time and tracked when the earliest-ending meeting finishes? That way, you know if the next meeting can reuse a room or needs a new one.

A min-heap (priority queue) keyed on end times lets you quickly check if the earliest-ending meeting has finished before the next meeting starts. If it has, pop it (reuse that room). If not, push the new meeting (allocate a new room).

Alternatively, separate all start times and end times into two sorted arrays. Walk through them with two pointers. Every start increments the room count, every end decrements it. The maximum count you hit is the answer.

title: Meeting Scheduler
description:
Given the availability time slots arrays `slots1` and `slots2` of two people and a meeting duration `duration`, return the **earliest time slot** that works for both of them and is of duration `duration`.
If there is no common time slot that satisfies the requirements, return an **empty array**.
The format of a time slot is an array of two elements `[start, end]` representing an inclusive time range from `start` to `end`.
It is guaranteed that no two availability slots of the same person intersect with each other. That is, for any two time slots `[start1, end1]` and `[start2, end2]` of the same person, either `start1 > end2` or `start2 > end1`.
##### **Example 1:**
**Input:** `slots1 = [[10,50],[60,120],[140,210]]`, `slots2 = [[0,15],[60,70]]`, `duration = 8`
**Output:** `[60,68]`
##### **Example 2:**
**Input:** `slots1 = [[10,50],[60,120],[140,210]]`, `slots2 = [[0,15],[60,70]]`, `duration = 12`
**Output:** `[]`
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= slots1.length, slots2.length <= 10^4`
- `slots1[i].length == 2`
- `slots2[i].length == 2`
- `0 <= slots1[i][0] < slots1[i][1] <= 10^9`
- `0 <= slots2[i][0] < slots2[i][1] <= 10^9`
- `1 <= duration <= 10^6`
constraints:
1 <= slots1.length, slots2.length <= 10^4
slots1[i].length == 2
slots2[i].length == 2
0 <= slots1[i][0] < slots1[i][1] <= 10^9
0 <= slots2[i][0] < slots2[i][1] <= 10^9
1 <= duration <= 10^6
hints: A valid meeting time must fall within the overlap of one slot from each person. How do you compute the overlap of two intervals?
The overlap of intervals [s1, e1] and [s2, e2] is [max(s1, s2), min(e1, e2)]. If max(s1, s2) < min(e1, e2), the overlap is non-empty.

If you sort both slot lists by start time, you can use two pointers to walk through them in order, similar to merging two sorted arrays. At each step, advance the pointer whose current slot ends earlier.
At each pair of overlapping slots, check if the overlap length is at least duration. If so, the meeting starts at max(s1, s2) and ends at max(s1, s2) + duration.

title: Interval List Intersections
description:
You are given two lists of closed intervals, `firstList` and `secondList`, where `firstList[i] = [starti, endi]` and `secondList[j] = [startj, endj]`. Each list of intervals is pairwise **disjoint** and in **sorted order**.
Return the intersection of these two interval lists.
A **closed interval** `[a, b]` (with `a <= b`) denotes the set of real numbers `x` with `a <= x <= b`.
The **intersection** of two closed intervals is a set of real numbers that is either empty, or represented as a closed interval. For example, the intersection of `[1, 3]` and `[2, 4]` is `[2, 3]`.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Example 3:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `0 <= firstList.length, secondList.length <= 1000`
- `firstList.length + secondList.length >= 1`
- `0 <= starti < endi <= 10^9`
- `0 <= startj < endj <= 10^9`
- `starti < starti+1`
- `startj < startj+1`
constraints:
`0 <= firstList.length, secondList.length <= 1000`
`firstList.length + secondList.length >= 1`
`0 <= starti < endi <= 109`
`endi < starti+1`
`0 <= startj < endj <= 109 `
`endj < startj+1`
hints: Two intervals [a, b] and [c, d] intersect if and only if a <= d and c <= b. The intersection itself is [max(a, c), min(b, d)].
Since both lists are sorted by start time, you can use two pointers to compare one interval from each list at a time.

After checking two intervals for overlap, which pointer should you advance? Think about which interval ends first. The one that ends earlier can't possibly overlap with anything else from the other list.
Always advance the pointer whose current interval has the smaller end value. This guarantees you never miss an intersection.

title: Car Pooling
description:
There is a car with `capacity` empty seats. The vehicle only drives east (i.e., it cannot turn around and drive west).
You are given the integer `capacity` and an array `trips`, where `trips[i] = [numPassengersi, fromi, toi]` indicates that the `i`th trip has `numPassengersi` passengers and the locations of pick-up and drop-off are `fromi` and `toi` respectively.
The passengers are picked up at `fromi` and dropped off at `toi`. Each location is a number of kilometers east of the car's starting position.
Return `true` if it is possible to pick up and drop off all passengers for all the given trips, or `false` otherwise.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Example 3:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= trips.length <= 1000`
- `trips[i].length == 3`
- `1 <= numPassengersi <= 100`
- `0 <= fromi < toi <= 1000`
- `1 <= capacity <= 10^5`
constraints:
`1 <= trips.length <= 1000`
`trips[i].length == 3`
`1 <= numPassengersi <= 100`
`0 <= fromi < toi <= 1000`
`1 <= capacity <= 105`
hints: Think about what happens at each location along the road. At some locations passengers get on, at others they get off. You only care whether the total passengers ever exceeds the capacity.
The location range is small (0 to 1000). Can you use an array where each index represents a location, and track how passengers change at each point?

For each trip [numPassengers, from, to], add numPassengers at index "from" and subtract numPassengers at index "to". Then walk through the array and compute a running sum. If the running sum ever exceeds capacity, return false.
This is the "difference array" or "line sweep" technique. The +/- events capture when passengers board and leave. The prefix sum reconstructs the actual passenger count at each location.

title: Merge Intervals
description:
Given an array of `intervals` where `intervals[i] = [start_i, end_i]`, merge all overlapping intervals, and return an array of the non-overlapping intervals that cover all the intervals in the input.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= intervals.length <= 10^4`
- `intervals[i].length == 2`
- `0 <= start_i <= end_i <= 10^4`
constraints:
`1 <= intervals.length <= 104`
`intervals[i].length == 2`
`0 <= starti <= endi <= 104`
hints: If the intervals were sorted by their start times, how would that simplify detecting overlaps?
After sorting, two consecutive intervals overlap if the start of the second interval is less than or equal to the end of the first.

As you iterate through sorted intervals, keep track of the current merged interval. If the next interval overlaps, extend the current interval's end. If it doesn't, finalize the current interval and start a new one.

When extending a merged interval, the new end should be the maximum of both intervals' ends, not just the second interval's end. Consider [1,10] and [2,5], where the first interval already extends past the second.

title: Insert Interval
description:
You are given an array of non-overlapping intervals `intervals` where `intervals[i] = [start_i, end_i]` represent the start and the end of the `i`th interval and `intervals` is sorted in ascending order by `start_i`. You are also given an interval `newInterval = [start, end]` that represents the start and end of another interval.
Insert `newInterval` into `intervals` such that `intervals` is still sorted in ascending order by `start_i` and `intervals` still does not have any overlapping intervals (merge overlapping intervals if necessary).
Return `intervals` after the insertion.
**Note** that you don't need to modify `intervals` in-place. You can make a new array and return it.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Example 3:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `0 <= intervals.length <= 10^4`
- `intervals[i].length == 2`
- `0 <= start_i <= end_i <= 10^5`
- `intervals` is sorted by `start_i` in ascending order.
- `newInterval.length == 2`
- `0 <= start <= end <= 10^5`
constraints:
`0 <= intervals.length <= 104`
`intervals[i].length == 2`
`0 <= starti <= endi <= 105`
`intervals` is sorted by `starti` in **ascending** order.
`newInterval.length == 2`
`0 <= start <= end <= 105`
hints: Since the intervals are already sorted, think about what makes an interval come "before," "overlap with," or "after" the new interval.
An existing interval [a, b] comes entirely before the new interval [s, e] if b < s. It comes entirely after if a > e. Everything else overlaps.
When intervals overlap, the merged result has start = min(all starts) and end = max(all ends). You can update the new interval's boundaries as you find overlapping intervals.
Process in three phases: add all intervals before the new one, merge all overlapping intervals with the new one, then add all intervals after it.

title: Remove Covered Intervals
description:
Given an array `intervals` where `intervals[i] = [l_i, r_i]` represent the interval `[l_i, r_i)`, remove all intervals that are covered by another interval in the list.
The interval `[a, b)` is covered by the interval `[c, d)` if and only if `c <= a` and `b <= d`.
Return the number of remaining intervals.
##### **Example 1:**
**Explanation:** Interval [3,6] is covered by [2,8], so it is removed. The remaining intervals are [1,4] and [2,8].
##### **Example 2:**
**Explanation:** [2,3] is covered by [1,4], so only [1,4] remains.
##### **Example 3:**
**Explanation:** [1,2] is covered by [1,4], and [3,4] is covered by [1,4]. Only [1,4] remains.
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= intervals.length <= 1000`
- `intervals[i].length == 2`
- `0 <= l_i < r_i <= 10^5`
- All the given intervals are unique.
constraints:
`1 <= intervals.length <= 1000`
`intervals[i].length == 2`
`0 <= li < ri <= 105`
All the given intervals are **unique**.
hints: Think about what ordering of the intervals would make it easy to identify which ones are covered.
If you sort intervals by their start point, then any interval that covers another must appear before (or at the same position as) the covered interval. But how do you handle ties in the start point?

Sort by start ascending, and when starts are equal, sort by end descending. This way, the widest interval with a given start comes first. Then a single pass tracking the maximum end seen so far lets you identify covered intervals.
After sorting, iterate through the intervals. If the current interval's end is less than or equal to the maximum end seen so far, it is covered. Otherwise, it survives and you update the maximum end.

title: Partition Labels
description:
You are given a string `s`. We want to partition the string into as many parts as possible so that each letter appears in at most one part.
Note that the partition is done so that after concatenating all the parts in order, the resultant string should be `s`.
Return a list of integers representing the size of these parts.
##### **Example 1:**
**Explanation:** The partition is "ababcbaca", "defegde", "hijhklij". This is a partition so that each letter appears in at most one part. A partition like "ababcbacadefegde", "hijhklij" is incorrect, because it splits `s` into fewer parts.
##### **Example 2:**
**Explanation:** Since 'e' appears at both the start and end of the string, the entire string must be one partition.
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= s.length <= 500`
- `s` consists of lowercase English letters.
constraints:
`1 <= s.length <= 500`
`s` consists of lowercase English letters.
hints: For each character in the string, what is the furthest right it appears? How does that constrain where a partition can end?
If you know the last occurrence of every character, you can scan left to right and track the furthest reach of the current partition. When does a partition end?

A partition ends at index i when i equals the maximum last occurrence of all characters seen so far in the current partition. At that point, no character from this partition appears later, so it is safe to cut.

title: Minimum Number of Arrows to Burst Balloons
description:
There are some spherical balloons taped onto a flat wall that represents the XY-plane. The balloons are represented as a 2D integer array `points` where `points[i] = [x_start, x_end]` denotes a balloon whose horizontal diameter stretches between `x_start` and `x_end`. You do not know the exact y-coordinates of the balloons.
Arrows can be shot up directly vertically (in the positive y-direction) from different points along the x-axis. A balloon with `x_start` and `x_end` is burst by an arrow shot at `x` if `x_start <= x <= x_end`. There is no limit to the number of arrows that can be shot. A shot arrow keeps traveling up infinitely, bursting any balloons in its path.
Given the array `points`, return the minimum number of arrows that must be shot to burst all the balloons.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Example 3:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= points.length <= 10^5`
- `points[i].length == 2`
- `-2^31 <= x_start < x_end <= 2^31 - 1`
constraints:
`1 <= points.length <= 105`
`points[i].length == 2`
`-231 <= xstart < xend <= 231 - 1`
hints: Think about what happens if you sort the balloons. What ordering would make it easiest to decide which balloons can share an arrow?
If you sort by the end point of each balloon, the first balloon in sorted order must be burst. Where is the optimal place to shoot that arrow?
Shoot the arrow at the end point of the first balloon (in sorted order). This gives the best chance of also bursting subsequent balloons. Then skip all balloons that this arrow already bursts.
A subsequent balloon is burst by the current arrow if its start point is at or before the arrow's position (the current end point). Once you find one that isn't, you need a new arrow.

title: Maximum Number of Events That Can Be Attended
description:
You are given an array of `events` where `events[i] = [startDayi, endDayi]`. Every event `i` starts at `startDayi` and ends at `endDayi`.
You can attend an event `i` at any day `d` where `startDayi <= d <= endDayi`. You can only attend one event at any given day `d`.
Return the maximum number of events you can attend.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= events.length <= 10^5`
- `events[i].length == 2`
- `1 <= startDayi <= endDayi <= 10^5`
constraints:
`1 <= events.length <= 105`
`events[i].length == 2`
`1 <= startDayi <= endDayi <= 105`
hints: Think about processing events day by day. On each day, which available events could you attend?
If multiple events are available on the same day, which one should you attend first? Consider which event has the least flexibility for future days.
Sort events by start day. As you move through days, add newly available events to a min-heap keyed by end day. Always attend the event ending soonest.
Before picking an event from the heap on a given day, remove any events whose end day has already passed. They are no longer attendable.

title: Non-overlapping Intervals
description:
