`1 <= nums[i] <= 104`
The frequency of each element is in the range `[1, 4]`.
hints: Start by checking if the total sum is divisible by k. If not, you can immediately return false. If it is, each subset must sum to totalSum / k.
Try thinking of this as filling k buckets, each with a target capacity. Use backtracking to assign each number to one of the k buckets.
Sorting the array in descending order and trying larger numbers first can dramatically improve pruning. If a large number exceeds the target, you know right away it's impossible.
With n <= 16, you can represent which elements have been used as a bitmask. This lets you use DP where the state is the set of elements already assigned to subsets.

title: Minimum Number of Work Sessions to Finish the Tasks
description:
There are `n` tasks assigned to you. The task times are represented as an integer array `tasks` of length `n`, where the `i`th task takes `tasks[i]` hours to finish. A work session is when you work for **at most** `sessionTime` consecutive hours and then take a break.
You should finish the given tasks in a way that satisfies the following conditions:
- If you start a task in a work session, you must complete it in the **same** work session.
- You can start a new task **immediately** after finishing the previous one.
- You may complete the tasks in **any order**.
Given `tasks` and `sessionTime`, return the **minimum number of work sessions** needed to finish all the tasks.
##### **Example 1:**
You can finish the tasks in two work sessions. Assign tasks [1, 2] to session 1 (1 + 2 = 3 <= 3) and task [3] to session 2 (3 <= 3).
##### **Example 2:**
You can finish the tasks in two work sessions. Assign tasks [3, 1, 3] to session 1 (3 + 1 + 3 = 7 <= 8) and tasks [1, 1] to session 2 (1 + 1 = 2 <= 8).
##### **Example 3:**
You can finish all tasks in one work session since 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5 = 15 <= 15.
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= n <= 14`
- `1 <= tasks[i] <= 10`
- `max(tasks[i]) <= sessionTime <= 15`
constraints:
`n == tasks.length`
`1 <= n <= 14`
`1 <= tasks[i] <= 10`
`max(tasks[i]) <= sessionTime <= 15`
hints: Since n is at most 14, think about representing the set of completed tasks as a bitmask. What information do you need besides which tasks are done?

For each state (set of completed tasks), you also need to know how much time is left in the current session. This determines whether the next task fits in the current session or requires starting a new one.
Define dp[mask] as a pair: (minimum sessions, maximum remaining time in the current session). For each mask, try adding each unfinished task and update the state accordingly.

When adding a task, if it fits in the remaining time, use the current session. Otherwise, start a new session (increment sessions by 1, reset remaining time to sessionTime - task time). Among all choices, pick the one that minimizes sessions first, then maximizes remaining time.

title: Fair Distribution of Cookies
description:
You are given an integer array `cookies`, where `cookies[i]` denotes the number of cookies in the `i`th bag. You are also given an integer `k` that denotes the number of children to distribute **all** the bags of cookies to. All the cookies in the same bag must go to the same child and cannot be split up.
The **unfairness** of a distribution is defined as the **maximum** total cookies obtained by a single child in the distribution.
Return the **minimum** unfairness of all distributions.
##### **Example 1:**
One optimal distribution is [8,15,8] and [10,20]. The unfairness is max(31, 30) = 31.
##### **Example 2:**
One optimal distribution is [6,1], [3,2,2], and [4,1,2]. The unfairness is max(7, 7, 7) = 7.
##### **Constraints:**
- `2 <= cookies.length <= 8`
- `1 <= cookies[i] <= 10^5`
- `2 <= k <= cookies.length`
constraints:
`2 <= cookies.length <= 8`
`1 <= cookies[i] <= 105`
`2 <= k <= cookies.length`
hints: Think about assigning each bag to one of k children. With at most 8 bags, you can try all possible assignments using backtracking.
To prune the search, if any child's current total already exceeds your best known answer, stop exploring that branch.

Children with zero cookies assigned are interchangeable. If child 0 and child 1 both have zero cookies so far, assigning the current bag to child 0 vs child 1 produces the same result. Only try one of them.
Alternatively, think about using bitmask DP. Precompute the sum for every subset of bags, then figure out how to partition all bags into k groups to minimize the maximum group sum.

title: Shortest Path Visiting All Nodes
description:
You have an undirected, connected graph of `n` nodes labeled from `0` to `n - 1`. You are given an array `graph` where `graph[i]` is a list of all the nodes connected with node `i` by an edge.
Return the length of the shortest path that visits every node. You may start and stop at any node, you may revisit nodes, and you may reuse edges.
##### **Example 1:**
**Explanation:** One possible path is `[1,0,2,0,3]`. We start at node 1, go to 0, then to 2, back to 0, then to 3. That's 4 edges total.
##### **Example 2:**
**Explanation:** One possible path is `[0,1,4,2,3]`. This path visits all 5 nodes in 4 edges.
##### **Constraints:**
- `n == graph.length`
- `1 <= n <= 12`
- `0 <= graph[i].length < n`
- `graph[i]` does not contain `i`.
- If `graph[a]` contains `b`, then `graph[b]` contains `a`.
- The input graph is always connected.
constraints:
`n == graph.length`
`1 <= n <= 12`
`0 <= graph[i].length < n`
`graph[i]` does not contain `i`.
If `graph[a]` contains `b`, then `graph[b]` contains `a`.
The input graph is always connected.
hints: With n â‰¤ 12, you can represent the set of visited nodes as a bitmask. Bit i being set means node i has been visited.

The state of your search isn't just which node you're at. It's which node you're at AND which nodes you've visited so far. Think of this as a BFS over a state space of (currentNode, visitedMask) pairs.
Since you can start at any node, initialize your BFS queue with every node as a possible starting point, each with a visited mask that has only that node's bit set.
The target state is any (node, mask) where the mask has all n bits set, meaning every node has been visited. The first time you reach such a state, the BFS distance is your answer.

title: Count Numbers with Unique Digits
description:
Given an integer `n`, return the count of all numbers with unique digits, `x`, where `0 <= x < 10^n`.
##### **Example 1:**
*The answer should be the total numbers in the range [0, 100) excluding 11, 22, 33, 44, 55, 66, 77, 88, and 99, which have at least one repeated digit. There are 91 numbers with all unique digits.*
##### **Example 2:**
*Only the number 0 is in the range [0, 1).*
##### **Example 3:**
*All single-digit numbers (0 through 9) have unique digits.*
##### **Constraints:**
- `0 <= n <= 8`
hints: Think about how many k-digit numbers can have all unique digits. A 1-digit number always has unique digits. What about 2-digit numbers?

For a k-digit number, the first digit has 9 choices (1 through 9, since we cannot have a leading zero). The second digit has 9 choices (0 through 9 minus the first digit). The third has 8 choices, and so on.
The count of k-digit numbers with unique digits is 9 * 9 * 8 * 7 * ... * (11 - k). Sum this for k from 1 to n, and add 1 for the number 0.
Since we only have 10 distinct digits (0-9), any number with more than 10 digits must have a repeated digit. So for n > 10, the answer is the same as for n = 10.

title: Number of Digit One
description:
Given an integer `n`, count the total number of digit `1` appearing in all non-negative integers less than or equal to `n`.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `0 <= n <= 10^9`
hints: Instead of counting 1s number by number, try counting how many times digit 1 appears at each position (ones, tens, hundreds, ...) across all numbers from 0 to n.

For a given digit position, the count of 1s depends on three things: the digits to the left of that position (the "higher" part), the digit at that position (the "current" digit), and the digits to the right (the "lower" part).

Consider the tens place for n = 3141. The higher part is 31, the current digit is 4, and the lower part is 1. Since the current digit is greater than 1, the number of 1s in the tens place is (31 + 1) * 10 = 320. Can you generalize this for when the current digit is 0 or exactly 1?

There are exactly three cases: if the current digit is 0, the count is higher * factor. If the current digit is 1, the count is higher * factor + lower + 1. If the current digit is greater than 1, the count is (higher + 1) * factor. Here, "factor" is the place value (1, 10, 100, ...).

title: Numbers At Most N Given Digit Set
description:
Given an array of `digits` which is sorted in **non-decreasing** order. You can write numbers using each `digits[i]` as many times as we want. For example, if `digits = ["1","3","5"]`, we may write numbers such as `"13"`, `"551"`, and `"1351315"`.
Return the number of positive integers that can be generated that are less than or equal to `n`.
##### **Example 1:**
The 20 numbers that can be written are: 1, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 15, 17, 31, 33, 35, 37, 51, 53, 55, 57, 71, 73, 75, 77.
##### **Example 2:**
We can write 3 one-digit numbers, 9 two-digit numbers, 27 three-digit numbers, and so on. In total, this is 29523 integers.
##### **Example 3:**
The only number we can write is 7, and 7 <= 8.
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= digits.length <= 9`
- `digits[i].length == 1`
- `digits[i]` is a digit from `'1'` to `'9'`
- All the values in `digits` are **unique**
- `digits` is sorted in **non-decreasing** order
- `1 <= n <= 10^9`
constraints:
`1 <= digits.length <= 9`
`digits[i].length == 1`
`digits[i]` is a digit from `'1'` to `'9'`.
All the values in `digits` are **unique**.
`digits` is sorted in **non-decreasing** order.
`1 <= n <= 109`
hints: Think about numbers with fewer digits than n separately from numbers with the same number of digits as n. How many k-digit numbers can you form using d allowed digits?
For k-digit numbers (where k is less than the number of digits in n), every combination is valid. That gives d^k numbers for each such k.

For numbers with the same number of digits as n, process digit by digit from left to right. At each position, count how many allowed digits are strictly less than the corresponding digit of n, then check if you can match the current digit exactly and continue.
If the current digit of n exactly matches an allowed digit, you move to the next position with the constraint still tight. If no allowed digit matches the current digit of n, you stop.

title: Knight Probability in Chessboard
description:
On an `n x n` chessboard, a knight starts at the cell `(row, column)` and attempts to make exactly `k` moves. The rows and columns are 0-indexed, so the top-left cell is `(0, 0)`, and the bottom-right cell is `(n - 1, n - 1)`.
A chess knight has eight possible moves it can make, as illustrated below. Each move is two cells in a cardinal direction, then one cell in an orthogonal direction.
Each time the knight is to move, it chooses one of eight possible moves uniformly at random (even if the piece would go off the chessboard) and moves there.
The knight continues moving until it has made exactly `k` moves or has moved off the chessboard.
Return the probability that the knight remains on the board after it has stopped moving.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= n <= 25`
- `0 <= k <= 100`
- `0 <= row, column <= n - 1`
hints: Think of this as a probability distribution problem. At step 0, the knight is at (row, column) with probability 1. After each move, the probability spreads to the 8 possible knight destinations.
Define dp[step][r][c] as the probability of the knight being at cell (r, c) after exactly step moves. Can you write the transition from step to step+1?

At each step, for every cell (r, c) that has nonzero probability, distribute probability/8 to each of the 8 knight-move destinations that are still on the board. Destinations off the board lose that probability.
You only need the probabilities from the previous step to compute the current step. This means you can optimize from O(k*n^2) space to O(n^2) using two 2D arrays.

title: Soup Servings
description:
There are two types of soup: **type A** and **type B**. Initially, we have `n` ml of each type of soup. There are four kinds of operations:
- Serve 100 ml of soup A and 0 ml of soup B,
- Serve 75 ml of soup A and 25 ml of soup B,
- Serve 50 ml of soup A and 50 ml of soup B,
- Serve 25 ml of soup A and 75 ml of soup B.
When we serve some soup, we give it to someone, and we no longer have it. Each turn, we will choose from the four operations with an equal probability 0.25. If the remaining volume of soup is not enough to complete the operation, we will serve as much as we can. We stop once we no longer have some quantity of both types of soup.
Note that we do not have an operation where all 100 ml's of soup B are used first.
Return the probability that soup A will be empty first, plus half the probability that A and B become empty at the same time. Answers within `10^-5` of the actual answer will be accepted.
##### **Example 1:**
**Explanation:** If we choose the first two operations, A will become empty first. For the third operation, both A and B become empty at the same time. For the fourth operation, B will become empty first. So the total probability = 0.25 * (1 + 1 + 0.5 + 0) = 0.625.
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `0 <= n <= 10^9`
hints: Since all serving amounts are multiples of 25, you can simplify by dividing n by 25 and working with smaller units. This reduces the state space significantly.
Think of this as a 2D state problem where each state is (remaining A, remaining B). From each state, you transition to four new states with equal probability.
On average, soup A loses more per turn than soup B. So as n grows, the probability that A empties first approaches 1.0. For large enough n, you can just return 1.0.
Memoize the recursive probability computation. The base cases are: if A is empty and B is not, return 1.0. If both are empty, return 0.5. If B is empty and A is not, return 0.0.

title: New 21 Game
description:
Alice plays the following game, which is loosely based on the card game "21".
Alice starts with `0` points and draws numbers while she has less than `k` points. During each draw, she gains an integer number of points randomly from the range `[1, maxPts]`, where `maxPts` is an integer. Each draw is independent and the outcomes have equal probabilities.
Alice stops drawing numbers when she gets `k` or more points.
Return the probability that Alice has `n` or fewer points.
##### **Example 1:**
**Input:** `n = 10`, `k = 1`, `maxPts = 10`
**Output:** `1.00000`
**Explanation:** Alice gets a single card, then stops. She can get 1 through 10 points, all of which are <= 10.
##### **Example 2:**
**Input:** `n = 6`, `k = 1`, `maxPts = 10`
**Output:** `0.60000`
**Explanation:** Alice gets a single card, then stops. She has a 6/10 chance of getting 6 or fewer points.
##### **Example 3:**
**Input:** `n = 21`, `k = 17`, `maxPts = 10`
**Output:** `0.73278`
**Explanation:** Alice draws cards until she has 17 or more points. The probability she ends with 21 or fewer is approximately 0.73278.
##### **Constraints:**
- `0 <= k <= n <= 10^4`
- `1 <= maxPts <= 10^4`
hints: Think about the probability of Alice landing on each possible score. If you know the probability of being at each score less than k, you can figure out the probability of reaching each final score.

Define dp[i] as the probability that Alice has exactly i points at some moment. For i < k, Alice has not stopped yet and will draw again. For i >= k, Alice has stopped. The probability of reaching score i is the sum of probabilities of reaching each score j where i - maxPts <= j < k, times 1/maxPts.
The recurrence involves summing dp[i-1] + dp[i-2] + ... + dp[i-maxPts]. Instead of recomputing this sum each time, maintain a running sum and add/subtract as you slide the window forward.
Be careful about the window boundaries. Only scores less than k contribute to future scores (Alice stops at k or above). So when computing dp[i], you add dp[i-1] to the window only if i-1 < k.

title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock with Cooldown
description:
You are given an array `prices` where `prices[i]` is the price of a given stock on the `i`th day.
Find the maximum profit you can achieve. You may complete as many transactions as you like (i.e., buy one and sell one share of the stock multiple times) with the following restrictions:
- After you sell your stock, you cannot buy stock on the next day (i.e., cooldown of one day).
**Note:** You may not engage in multiple transactions simultaneously (i.e., you must sell the stock before you buy again).
##### **Example 1:**
**Explanation:** transactions = [buy, sell, cooldown, buy, sell]
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= prices.length <= 5000`
- `0 <= prices[i] <= 1000`
constraints:
`1 <= prices.length <= 5000`
`0 <= prices[i] <= 1000`
hints: On any given day, you're in one of three situations: holding a stock, not holding (and free to buy), or in a cooldown after selling. Think about what transitions are possible from each.
Define three DP states for each day: the best profit if you're holding stock, if you're free to buy, and if you just sold. Write recurrences for each state based on the previous day.
The cooldown means that after selling on day i, you can't buy on day i+1. So the "just sold" state on day i transitions to the "free to buy" state on day i+1, not directly back to "holding."
Each day only depends on the previous day's three states. You can collapse the arrays to three variables.

title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock with Transaction Fee
description:
You are given an array `prices` where `prices[i]` is the price of a given stock on the `i`th day, and an integer `fee` representing a transaction fee.
Find the maximum profit you can achieve. You may complete as many transactions as you like, but you need to pay the transaction fee for each transaction.
**Note:**
- A transaction is a buy followed by a sell.
- You may not engage in multiple transactions simultaneously (i.e., you must sell the stock before you buy again).
- The transaction fee is only charged once for each transaction (not per buy and per sell).
##### **Example 1:**
**Explanation:** The maximum profit can be achieved by buying on day 0 (price = 1), selling on day 3 (price = 8), buying on day 4 (price = 4), and selling on day 5 (price = 9). The total profit is ((8 - 1) - 2) + ((9 - 4) - 2) = 8.
##### **Example 2:**
**Explanation:** The maximum profit can be achieved by buying on day 0 (price = 1), selling on day 4 (price = 10). The total profit is (10 - 1) - 3 = 6.
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= prices.length <= 5 * 10^4`
- `1 <= prices[i] < 5 * 10^4`
- `0 <= fee < 5 * 10^4`
hints: At any point in time, you're in one of two states: either you're holding a stock, or you're not. Think about what decisions are available in each state.
Define two values for each day: the best profit you can have if you're holding a stock, and the best profit if you're not holding one. How do these relate to each other from one day to the next?
If you're not holding stock on day i, you either weren't holding yesterday (did nothing) or you sold today (had stock yesterday, sold at today's price minus the fee). Take the better option.
You only need the previous day's two values to compute today's values. This means you can reduce the space from O(n) to O(1).

title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock III
description:
You are given an array `prices` where `prices[i]` is the price of a given stock on the `i`th day.
Find the maximum profit you can achieve. You may complete **at most two transactions**. A transaction consists of buying one share and then selling it.
**Note:** You may not engage in multiple transactions simultaneously (i.e., you must sell the stock before you buy again).
##### **Example 1:**
**Explanation:** Buy on day 3 (price = 0) and sell on day 5 (price = 3), profit = 3 - 0 = 3. Then buy on day 6 (price = 1) and sell on day 7 (price = 4), profit = 4 - 1 = 3. Total profit = 3 + 3 = 6.
##### **Example 2:**
**Explanation:** Buy on day 0 (price = 1) and sell on day 4 (price = 5), profit = 5 - 1 = 4. One transaction achieves the maximum here.
##### **Example 3:**
**Explanation:** No profitable transaction is possible since prices only decrease.
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= prices.length <= 10^5`
- `0 <= prices[i] <= 10^5`
constraints:
`1 <= prices.length <= 105`
`0 <= prices[i] <= 105`
hints: Two non-overlapping transactions must have a boundary somewhere in the array. What if you split the array at every possible index and found the best single transaction on each side?
You can precompute the best single-transaction profit for the subarray prices[0..i] and prices[i..n-1] in O(n) each, using the Stock I technique.

Alternatively, think of it as a state machine. At any point, you are in one of these states: before any transaction, bought first stock, sold first stock, bought second stock, sold second stock. Track the best profit in each state as you iterate.
The state machine approach gives you four variables that update in a single pass. Since buy2 is computed from sell1, the second transaction builds on the first transaction's profit.

title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock IV
description:
You are given an integer array `prices` where `prices[i]` is the price of a given stock on the `i`th day, and an integer `k`.
Find the maximum profit you can achieve. You may complete **at most** `k` transactions. A transaction consists of buying one share and then selling it. You may not engage in multiple transactions simultaneously (i.e., you must sell the stock before you buy again).
##### **Example 1:**
**Explanation:** Buy on day 0 (price = 2) and sell on day 1 (price = 4), profit = 4 - 2 = 2.
##### **Example 2:**
**Explanation:** Buy on day 1 (price = 2) and sell on day 2 (price = 6), profit = 4. Then buy on day 4 (price = 0) and sell on day 5 (price = 3), profit = 3. Total profit = 7.
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= k <= 100`
- `1 <= prices.length <= 1000`
- `0 <= prices[i] <= 1000`
hints: At any point in time, your state is defined by three things: the current day, how many transactions you have completed so far, and whether you are holding a stock. Can you define a recursive function based on these states?

If you are holding a stock on day i, you have two choices: sell it or hold it. If you are not holding a stock, you can buy one (if you have transactions left) or do nothing. This gives you a recurrence relation.
The recurrence can be memoized with a 3D table: dp[day][transactions_used][holding]. But you can simplify to tracking buy[j] and sell[j] for each transaction j.
When k >= n/2, you can make as many transactions as you want. The problem reduces to the unlimited transactions case, which you can solve greedily in O(n).

title: Palindrome Number
description:
Given an integer `x`, return `true` if `x` is a palindrome, and `false` otherwise.
An integer is a palindrome when it reads the same forward and backward.
##### **Example 1:**
*121 reads as 121 from left to right and from right to left.*
##### **Example 2:**
*From left to right, it reads -121. From right to left it becomes 121-. Therefore it is not a palindrome.*
##### **Example 3:**
*Reads 01 from right to left. Therefore it is not a palindrome.*
##### **Constraints:**
- `-2^31 <= x <= 2^31 - 1`
**Follow up:** Could you solve it without converting the integer to a string?
hints: Negative numbers can never be palindromes. Neither can numbers ending in 0 (except 0 itself). Handle these cases first.
You can extract the last digit of a number using modulo 10, and remove it using integer division by 10.
What if you only reversed half of the number? You'd avoid potential overflow issues and know to stop when the reversed half is greater than or equal to the remaining half.

For a number like 1221, after reversing two digits you'd have reversed = 12 and x = 12. They're equal, so it's a palindrome. For 12321 (odd digits), reversed = 123 and x = 12, so check if x equals reversed / 10.

title: Plus One
description:
You are given a **large integer** represented as an integer array `digits`, where each `digits[i]` is the `i`th digit of the integer. The digits are ordered from most significant to least significant in left-to-right order. The large integer does not contain any leading `0`'s.
Increment the large integer by one and return the resulting array of digits.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Example 3:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= digits.length <= 100`
- `0 <= digits[i] <= 9`
- `digits` does not contain any leading `0`'s.
constraints:
`1 <= digits.length <= 100`
`0 <= digits[i] <= 9`
`digits` does not contain any leading `0`'s.
hints: Think about how you do addition by hand: start from the rightmost digit and work left, carrying over when a digit exceeds 9.
You only need to continue carrying when a digit becomes 10. As soon as you increment a digit and it stays at 9 or below, you can stop immediately.
The only case where the array grows in size is when every single digit is 9. In that case, the result is always 1 followed by all zeroes.
You don't need to convert the array to an integer. Work digit by digit from the end. If you finish the loop and still have a carry, prepend a 1.

title: Roman to Integer
description:
Roman numerals are represented by seven different symbols: `I`, `V`, `X`, `L`, `C`, `D` and `M`.
For example, `2` is written as `II` in Roman numeral, just two ones added together. `12` is written as `XII`, which is simply `X + II`. The number `27` is written as `XXVII`, which is `XX + V + II`.
Roman numerals are usually written largest to smallest from left to right. However, the numeral for four is not `IIII`. Instead, the number four is written as `IV`. Because the one is before the five we subtract it making four. The same principle applies to the number nine, which is written as `IX`. There are six instances where subtraction is used:
- `I` can be placed before `V` (5) and `X` (10) to make 4 and 9.
- `X` can be placed before `L` (50) and `C` (100) to make 40 and 90.
- `C` can be placed before `D` (500) and `M` (1000) to make 400 and 900.
Given a roman numeral, convert it to an integer.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Example 3:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= s.length <= 15`
- `s` contains only the characters `('I', 'V', 'X', 'L', 'C', 'D', 'M')`
- It is guaranteed that `s` is a valid roman numeral in the range `[1, 3999]`
constraints:
`1 <= s.length <= 15`
`s` contains only the characters `('I', 'V', 'X', 'L', 'C', 'D', 'M')`.
It is **guaranteed** that `s` is a valid roman numeral in the range `[1, 3999]`.
hints: Start by creating a mapping from each Roman numeral character to its integer value. How would you process the string if every character was simply added?
The subtraction cases (like IV, IX, XL) always involve a smaller value appearing immediately before a larger value. Can you detect this by comparing adjacent characters?

As you iterate through the string, compare the current character's value to the next character's value. If the current is smaller, subtract it instead of adding it. This single rule handles all six subtraction cases.
You can also process the string from right to left. Keep track of the previous value you saw. If the current value is smaller than the previous, subtract it.

title: Excel Sheet Column Title
description:
Given an integer `columnNumber`, return its corresponding column title as it appears in an Excel sheet.
For example:
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Example 3:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= columnNumber <= 2^31 - 1`
constraints: `1 <= columnNumber <= 231 - 1`
hints: Think of this as a number base conversion problem. What base are you converting to?
It is similar to base 26, but the digits range from 1 to 26 (A to Z) instead of 0 to 25. How can you adjust the number before taking the modulo to account for this 1-indexing?
Subtract 1 from the column number before each modulo operation. This shifts the range from 1-26 to 0-25, which maps directly to A-Z. Then divide by 26 and repeat.
You will build the string in reverse order (rightmost character first). Either prepend each character or reverse the string at the end.

title: Greatest Common Divisor of Strings
description:
For two strings `s` and `t`, we say "`t` divides `s`" if and only if `s = t + t + t + ... + t` (i.e., `t` is concatenated with itself one or more times to form `s`).
Given two strings `str1` and `str2`, return the largest string `x` such that `x` divides both `str1` and `str2`.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Example 3:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= str1.length, str2.length <= 1000`
- `str1` and `str2` consist of uppercase English letters.
constraints:
`1 <= str1.length, str2.length <= 1000`
`str1` and `str2` consist of English uppercase letters.
hints: If a string x divides both str1 and str2, what can you say about the length of x relative to the lengths of str1 and str2?
The length of the GCD string must divide both len(str1) and len(str2). So the length of the answer is at most gcd(len(str1), len(str2)).
How can you quickly check whether a GCD string even exists? Think about what happens when you concatenate str1 + str2 versus str2 + str1.
If str1 + str2 equals str2 + str1, then the GCD string is the prefix of length gcd(len(str1), len(str2)). If they are not equal, no GCD string exists.

title: Ugly Number
description:
An **ugly number** is a positive integer whose prime factors are limited to `2`, `3`, and `5`.
Given an integer `n`, return `true` if `n` is an **ugly number**.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Example 3:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `-2^31 <= n <= 2^31 - 1`
hints: Think about what happens if you keep dividing the number by 2, 3, and 5. What should you be left with if the number is ugly?
If after removing all factors of 2, 3, and 5 the number becomes 1, it's ugly. If anything else remains, it has a prime factor that isn't 2, 3, or 5.
Don't forget the edge cases: negative numbers and zero are never ugly. And 1 is always ugly.

title: Ugly Number II
description:
An **ugly number** is a positive integer whose prime factors are limited to `2`, `3`, and `5`.
Given an integer `n`, return the `n`th ugly number.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= n <= 1690`
hints: Every ugly number (beyond 1) is produced by multiplying a previous ugly number by 2, 3, or 5. Can you use this fact to generate them in order?
If you had a min-heap, you could always extract the smallest candidate and then push its multiples (times 2, 3, 5) back into the heap. But watch out for duplicates.
Instead of a heap, think about maintaining three pointers into the ugly number sequence itself. Each pointer tracks which ugly number should next be multiplied by 2, 3, or 5.

At each step, the next ugly number is the minimum of ugly[i2]*2, ugly[i3]*3, and ugly[i5]*5. After picking the minimum, advance whichever pointer(s) produced that value. Advancing multiple pointers when there's a tie handles duplicates.

title: Reverse Integer
description:
Given a signed 32-bit integer `x`, return `x` with its digits reversed. If reversing `x` causes the value to go outside the signed 32-bit integer range `[-2^31, 2^31 - 1]`, then return `0`.
**Assume the environment does not allow you to store 64-bit integers (signed or unsigned).**
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Example 3:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `-2^31 <= x <= 2^31 - 1`
hints: You can extract the last digit of a number using the modulo operator (x % 10), and remove it using integer division (x / 10).
Build the reversed number one digit at a time: multiply the running result by 10 and add the next digit. Think about how a number is constructed in base 10.

The tricky part is overflow detection. Before multiplying by 10 and adding a digit, check if doing so would exceed Integer.MAX_VALUE or go below Integer.MIN_VALUE. Compare against MAX_VALUE / 10 to catch it before it happens.

If the current result equals MAX_VALUE / 10 exactly, you also need to check whether the next digit would push it over. MAX_VALUE ends in 7, so any digit greater than 7 would overflow. Similarly, MIN_VALUE ends in -8.

title: Multiply Strings
description:
Given two non-negative integers `num1` and `num2` represented as strings, return the product of `num1` and `num2`, also represented as a string.
**Note:** You must not use any built-in BigInteger library or convert the inputs to integer directly.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= num1.length, num2.length <= 200`
- `num1` and `num2` consist of digits only.
- `num1` and `num2` do not contain any leading zeroes, except the number 0 itself.
constraints:
`1 <= num1.length, num2.length <= 200`
`num1` and `num2` consist of digits only.
Both `num1` and `num2` do not contain any leading zero, except the number `0` itself.
hints: Think about how you multiply numbers by hand. You multiply each digit of one number by each digit of the other, keeping track of the position (ones place, tens place, hundreds place, etc.).
When you multiply digit at index i of num1 by digit at index j of num2, the result lands at a specific position in the product. What position is that?

The product of digits at positions i and j (from the left) contributes to position i + j + 1 in the result. You can use an array of size num1.length + num2.length to accumulate all partial products, handling carries afterward.
Process carries from right to left: each position keeps its value mod 10, and the carry (value / 10) gets added to the next position. Then convert the array to a string, skipping leading zeroes.

title: Find the Duplicate Number
description:
Given an array of integers `nums` containing `n + 1` integers where each integer is in the range `[1, n]` inclusive.
There is only one repeated number in `nums`, return this repeated number.
You must solve the problem **without** modifying the array `nums` and using only constant extra space.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Example 3:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= n <= 10^5`
- `nums.length == n + 1`
- `1 <= nums[i] <= n`
- All the integers in `nums` appear only once except for precisely one integer which appears two or more times.
constraints:
`1 <= n <= 105`
`nums.length == n + 1`
`1 <= nums[i] <= n`
All the integers in `nums` appear only **once** except for **precisely one integer** which appears **two or more** times.
hints: Think about the pigeonhole principle: if you have n+1 items in n containers, at least one container has more than one item. Can you use the value range [1, n] to narrow down which "container" has the duplicate?

Consider binary search, but not on the array itself. What if you binary-searched on the value range [1, n]? For a candidate mid-point, count how many numbers in the array are less than or equal to mid. If the count exceeds mid, the duplicate must be in the lower half.

There is an O(n) approach. Since every value is in [1, n], you can treat nums[i] as a pointer to index nums[i]. This creates an implicit linked list. Because there is a duplicate, two indices point to the same value, which means there is a cycle. Floyd's tortoise and hare algorithm can detect it.

In Floyd's algorithm, after the slow and fast pointers meet inside the cycle, reset one pointer to the start (index 0) and advance both one step at a time. They will meet at the cycle entrance, which is the duplicate number.

title: Count Good Numbers
description:
A digit string is **good** if the digits (0-indexed) at **even** indices are **even** (0, 2, 4, 6, 8) and the digits at **odd** indices are **prime** (2, 3, 5, 7).
- For example, `"2582"` is good because the digits (`2` and `8`) at even positions are even and the digits (`5` and `2`) at odd positions are prime. However, `"3245"` is not good because `3` is at index 0 (an even index) but is not even.
Given an integer `n`, return the **total number of good digit strings of length** `n`. Since the answer may be large, return it **modulo** `10^9 + 7`.
A **digit string** is a string consisting of digits `0` through `9` that may contain leading zeros.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Example 3:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= n <= 10^15`
hints: Each position in the string is independent. How many choices does each even-indexed position have? How many choices does each odd-indexed position have?
The total count is 5^(number of even positions) * 4^(number of odd positions). Can you figure out how many even and odd positions a string of length n has?
With n up to 10^15, you cannot compute the power with a simple loop. Look into "fast power" or "binary exponentiation" to compute a^b mod m in O(log b) time.

For a string of length n, the number of even-indexed positions is ceil(n/2) and the number of odd-indexed positions is floor(n/2). Compute 5^ceil(n/2) * 4^floor(n/2) mod (10^9 + 7) using binary exponentiation.

title: Count Primes
description:
Given an integer `n`, return the number of prime numbers that are strictly less than `n`.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Example 3:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `0 <= n <= 5 * 10^6`
hints: Instead of checking whether each number is prime individually, think about how knowing one prime lets you eliminate many composite numbers at once.
If you know that 2 is prime, then 4, 6, 8, 10, ... are all not prime. If 3 is prime, then 9, 12, 15, ... are all not prime. Can you use this to "sieve out" composites?
You only need to mark multiples starting from p * p (not 2 * p), because smaller multiples like 2 * p, 3 * p, ... have already been marked by smaller primes.
You only need to sieve primes up to sqrt(n). Any composite number less than n must have a factor less than or equal to sqrt(n).

title: Factorial Trailing Zeroes
description:
Given an integer `n`, return the number of trailing zeroes in `n!`.
Note that `n! = n * (n - 1) * (n - 2) * ... * 3 * 2 * 1`.
##### **Example 1:**
**Explanation:** 3! = 6, which has no trailing zeroes.
##### **Example 2:**
**Explanation:** 5! = 120, which has one trailing zero.
##### **Example 3:**
**Explanation:** 0! = 1, which has no trailing zeroes.
##### **Constraints:**
- `0 <= n <= 10^4`
**Follow up:** Could you write a solution that works in logarithmic time complexity?
hints: A trailing zero is created by a factor of 10 in the product. What prime factors make up 10?
Since 10 = 2 x 5, you need pairs of (2, 5) in the prime factorization. Which factor is less common in n!?
Count how many multiples of 5 are in the range [1, n]. But be careful: 25 contributes two factors of 5, 125 contributes three, and so on.
The total count of factor 5 in n! is: floor(n/5) + floor(n/25) + floor(n/125) + ... Keep dividing until the denominator exceeds n.

title: Valid Triangle Number
description:
Given an integer array `nums`, return the number of triplets chosen from the array that can make triangles if we take them as side lengths of a triangle.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= nums.length <= 1000`
- `0 <= nums[i] <= 1000`
hints: Sorting the array first simplifies the triangle inequality check. If the array is sorted, which of the three inequality conditions can you skip?
After sorting, fix the largest side and figure out how many pairs of smaller sides can form a valid triangle with it.
For a fixed largest side nums[k], you need pairs (i, j) where i < j < k and nums[i] + nums[j] > nums[k]. Since the array is sorted, binary search or two pointers can find these pairs efficiently.
With two pointers: if nums[left] + nums[right] > nums[k], then every index between left and right also pairs with right to form a valid triangle. That lets you count multiple valid pairs in one step.

title: Valid Square
description:
Given the coordinates of four points in 2D space `p1`, `p2`, `p3`, and `p4`, return `true` if the four points construct a square.
The coordinate of a point `p_i` is represented as `[x_i, y_i]`. The input is **not** given in any order.
A **valid square** has four equal sides with positive length and four equal angles (each 90 degrees).
##### **Example 1:**
**Input:** `p1 = [0,0]`, `p2 = [1,1]`, `p3 = [1,0]`, `p4 = [0,1]`
**Output:** `true`
##### **Example 2:**
**Input:** `p1 = [0,0]`, `p2 = [1,1]`, `p3 = [1,0]`, `p4 = [0,2]`
**Output:** `false`
##### **Example 3:**
**Input:** `p1 = [1,0]`, `p2 = [-1,0]`, `p3 = [0,1]`, `p4 = [0,-1]`
**Output:** `true`
##### **Constraints:**
- `p1.length == p2.length == p3.length == p4.length == 2`
- `-10^4 <= x_i, y_i <= 10^4`
constraints:
`p1.length == p2.length == p3.length == p4.length == 2`
`-104 <= xi, yi <= 104`
hints: How many unique pairwise distances exist among four points? How many of each distance value should a valid square have?
A square has exactly 4 equal sides and 2 equal diagonals. The diagonal is always longer than the side. Use squared distances to avoid floating-point precision issues.

Compute all 6 pairwise squared distances between the 4 points. Sort them. For a valid square, the first 4 values should be equal (sides) and the last 2 should be equal (diagonals). Also check that the side length is not zero.

An alternative approach: try all 3 possible ways to pair the 4 points into 2 diagonal pairs. For each pairing, check if the two diagonals bisect each other, are equal in length, and are perpendicular.

title: Minimum Area Rectangle
description:
You are given an array of points in the **X-Y** plane. Return the minimum area of a rectangle formed from these points, with sides parallel to the X and Y axes. If there is not any such rectangle, return `0`.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= points.length <= 500`
- `points[i].length == 2`
- `0 <= xi, yi <= 4 * 10^4`
- All the given points are **unique**.
constraints:
`1 <= points.length <= 500`
`points[i].length == 2`
`0 <= xi, yi <= 4 * 104`
All the given points are **unique**.
hints: A rectangle with sides parallel to the axes is uniquely determined by two diagonal corners. If you know two opposite corners, you know all four.
Store all points in a set so you can check if a point exists in O(1). Then for each pair of points, check whether they could be diagonal corners of a rectangle.
Two points (x1, y1) and (x2, y2) are diagonal corners of an axis-aligned rectangle only if x1 != x2 AND y1 != y2. The other two corners would be (x1, y2) and (x2, y1).
An alternative approach: group points by their x-coordinate. For each pair of x-values, find the common y-coordinates. Any two common y-values form a rectangle.

title: Minimum Area Rectangle II
description:
You are given an array of points in the X-Y plane `points` where `points[i] = [xi, yi]`.
Return the minimum area of any rectangle formed from these points, with sides **not necessarily parallel** to the X and Y axes. If there is not any such rectangle, return `0`.
Answers within `10^-5` of the actual answer will be accepted.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Example 3:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= points.length <= 50`
- `points[i].length == 2`
- `0 <= xi, yi <= 4 * 10^4`
- All the given points are **unique**.
constraints:
`1 <= points.length <= 50`
`points[i].length == 2`
`0 <= xi, yi <= 4 * 104`
All the given points are **unique**.
hints: A rectangle's diagonals have a special property: they bisect each other. That means both diagonals share the same midpoint and have the same length.
Instead of checking all combinations of 4 points, try grouping pairs of points by their shared midpoint and diagonal length. Any two pairs in the same group can form a rectangle.

For two pairs of points (p1, p2) and (p3, p4) that share the same midpoint and diagonal length, the area of the rectangle they form is the cross product of the vectors from one corner to two adjacent corners.
A simpler brute force approach: for every triple of points, check if the fourth point needed to complete the rectangle exists in a hash set. Verify the right angle using dot product.

title: Max Points on a Line
description:
Given an array of `points` where `points[i] = [xi, yi]` represents a point on the X-Y plane, return the maximum number of points that lie on the same straight line.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Example 3:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= points.length <= 300`
- `points[i].length == 2`
- `-10^4 <= xi, yi <= 10^4`
- All the `points` are unique.
constraints:
`1 <= points.length <= 300`
`points[i].length == 2`
`-104 <= xi, yi <= 104`
All the `points` are **unique**.
hints: Two points always determine a line. How can you check if a third point lies on the same line as the first two?
If you fix one point as a reference, every other point defines a slope from it. Points with the same slope are collinear with the reference point.
Representing slopes as floating-point numbers can cause precision issues. Think about how to represent a slope exactly using two integers.
Store the slope as a reduced fraction (dy/gcd, dx/gcd) with a consistent sign convention. Use a hash map to count how many points share each slope from the reference point.

title: Repeated Substring Pattern
description:
Given a string `s`, check if it can be constructed by taking a substring of it and appending multiple copies of the substring together.
##### **Example 1:**
**Explanation:** It is the substring "ab" twice.
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Example 3:**
**Explanation:** It is the substring "abc" four times, or the substring "abcabc" twice.
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= s.length <= 10^4`
- `s` consists of lowercase English letters.
constraints:
`1 <= s.length <= 104`
`s` consists of lowercase English letters.
hints: If a string is made of a repeating pattern, the pattern's length must be a divisor of the total string length. This limits the number of candidate substrings you need to check.
You only need to check candidate substring lengths from 1 to n/2. For each candidate length that divides n, check if repeating that prefix produces the full string.
There is an O(n) approach: concatenate the string with itself (s + s), remove the first and last characters, and check if the original string exists in the result.

Think about why the concatenation trick works. If s has a repeating pattern of length k, then s + s creates a string where the original s appears at every offset that is a multiple of k, not just at positions 0 and n.

title: Repeated String Match
description:
Given two strings `a` and `b`, return the minimum number of times you should repeat string `a` so that string `b` is a substring of the repeated `a`. If it is not possible for `b` to be a substring of the repeated `a`, return `-1`.
Notice that string `"abc"` repeated 0 times is `""`, repeated 1 time is `"abc"`, and repeated 2 times is `"abcabc"`.
##### **Example 1:**
We return 3 because by repeating `a` three times `"abcdabcdabcd"`, b is a substring of it.
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= a.length, b.length <= 10^4`
- `a` and `b` consist of lowercase English letters.
constraints:
`1 <= a.length, b.length <= 104`
`a` and `b` consist of lowercase English letters.
hints: Think about the maximum number of repetitions you could ever need. How long does the repeated string need to be to guarantee b could fit inside it?

If b has length m and a has length n, you need at least ceil(m/n) copies of a to have enough characters. But you might need one more copy to handle the case where b starts partway through a copy of a.
Build the repeated string incrementally. Start with ceil(m/n) copies, check if b is a substring. If not, add one more copy and check again. If still not found, return -1.

For an optimized substring check, consider Rabin-Karp (rolling hash). Instead of checking every position with string comparison, compute a hash of b and slide a window across the repeated string, updating the hash in O(1) per position.

title: Number of Distinct Substrings in a String
description:
Given a string `s`, return the number of **distinct substrings** of `s`.
A **substring** is a contiguous sequence of characters within a string. The empty string `""` is **not** counted as a substring.
##### **Example 1:**
**Explanation:** The 21 distinct substrings are: "a", "b", "aa", "ab", "bb", "ba", "aab", "abb", "bba", "bab", "aba", "aabb", "abba", "bbab", "baba", "aabba", "abbab", "bbaba", "aabbab", "abbaba", "aabbaba".
##### **Example 2:**
**Explanation:** Since all characters are unique, every possible substring is distinct. For a string of length 7: 7 + 6 + 5 + 4 + 3 + 2 + 1 = 28.
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= s.length <= 500`
- `s` consists of lowercase English letters.
constraints:
1 <= s.length <= 500
s consists of lowercase English letters.
hints: Think about what data structure naturally handles prefix relationships between strings. Every substring starting at index i is a prefix of the suffix starting at index i.
If you insert all suffixes of the string into a Trie, each node in the Trie corresponds to a unique substring. Why?
As you insert a suffix character by character into the Trie, every time you create a new node, that represents a new distinct substring you haven't seen before.
Alternatively, you can use a HashSet and add every substring explicitly. With n up to 500, this produces at most ~125K substrings, which is manageable.

title: Longest Happy Prefix
description:
A string is called a **happy prefix** if it is a **non-empty** prefix which is also a suffix (excluding itself).
Given a string `s`, return the **longest happy prefix** of `s`. Return an empty string `""` if no such prefix exists.
##### **Example 1:**
**Explanation:** s contains 4 non-empty prefixes excluding itself ("l", "le", "lev", "leve"), and 4 non-empty suffixes excluding itself ("l", "el", "vel", "evel"). The largest prefix which is also a suffix is "l".
##### **Example 2:**
**Explanation:** "abab" is the largest prefix which is also a suffix. They can overlap in the original string.
##### **Example 3:**
**Explanation:** There is no happy prefix for a single character string.
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= s.length <= 10^5`
- `s` contains only lowercase English letters.
constraints:
`1 <= s.length <= 105`
`s` contains only lowercase English letters.
hints: Think about what it means for a prefix to equal a suffix. You're comparing the first k characters with the last k characters. What's the most efficient way to do that without checking every possible k?
If you know about the KMP algorithm, recall the LPS (Longest Prefix Suffix) array. What does the last entry of the LPS array represent?
The LPS array at position i stores the length of the longest proper prefix of s[0..i] that is also a suffix. The answer to this problem is simply s[0..lps[n-1]-1], the first lps[n-1] characters.

If KMP feels unfamiliar, consider a rolling hash approach. You can maintain a hash of the prefix and a hash of the suffix as you extend both. When the hashes match, verify the actual strings. This gives O(n) expected time.

title: Shortest Palindrome
description:
You are given a string `s`. You can convert `s` to a palindrome by adding characters in front of it.
Return the shortest palindrome you can find by performing this transformation.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Example 3:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `0 <= s.length <= 5 * 10^4`
- `s` consists of lowercase English letters only.
constraints:
`0 <= s.length <= 5 * 104`
`s` consists of lowercase English letters only.
hints: The answer is always: reverse of some suffix of s, prepended to s. The shorter you want the answer, the longer the palindromic prefix you need to find.

Think about how to find the longest prefix of s that is also a palindrome. If you reverse s and call it rev, the longest palindromic prefix of s corresponds to a match between a prefix of s and a suffix of rev.

Construct the string s + "#" + reverse(s). The longest palindromic prefix of s equals the length of the longest proper prefix of this combined string that is also a suffix. That is exactly what the KMP failure function computes.

After computing the KMP failure table for s + "#" + reverse(s), the last value in the table tells you the length of the longest palindromic prefix. Everything after that prefix needs to be reversed and prepended.

title: Longest Duplicate Substring
description:
Given a string `s`, consider all duplicated substrings: (contiguous) substrings of `s` that occur 2 or more times. The occurrences may overlap.
Return any duplicated substring that has the longest possible length. If `s` does not have a duplicated substring, return `""`.
##### **Example 1:**
**Explanation:** The substring "ana" occurs twice (at index 1 and index 3). It is the longest duplicated substring.
##### **Example 2:**
**Explanation:** No substring occurs more than once, so the answer is an empty string.
##### **Constraints:**
- `2 <= s.length <= 3 * 10^4`
- `s` consists of lowercase English letters.
constraints:
`2 <= s.length <= 3 * 104`
`s` consists of lowercase English letters.
hints: If a duplicate substring of length k exists, does a duplicate substring of length k-1 also exist? What search technique does this monotonic property suggest?
Binary search on the length of the duplicate substring. For each candidate length, you need to efficiently check whether any substring of that length appears at least twice.
To check all substrings of a given length efficiently, use a rolling hash (Rabin-Karp). Slide a window of the candidate length across the string, computing each hash in O(1) using the previous hash.
Be careful with hash collisions. When two substrings have the same hash, verify they are actually equal by comparing the characters directly. Use a large prime modulus to minimize collisions.

title: Range Sum Query - Mutable
description:
Given an integer array `nums`, handle multiple queries of the following types:
- **Update** the value of an element in `nums`.
- Calculate the **sum** of the elements of `nums` between indices `left` and `right` **inclusive** where `left <= right`.
Implement the `NumArray` class:
- `NumArray(int[] nums)` Initializes the object with the integer array `nums`.
- `void update(int index, int val)` Updates the value of `nums[index]` to be `val`.
- `int sumRange(int left, int right)` Returns the sum of the elements of `nums` between indices `left` and `right` inclusive (i.e., `nums[left] + nums[left + 1] + ... + nums[right]`).
##### **Example 1:**
**Input:** `operations = ["NumArray", "sumRange", "update", "sumRange"]`, `arguments = [[[1,3,5]],[0,2],[1,2],[0,2]]`
**Output:** `[null, 9, null, 8]`
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= nums.length <= 3 * 10^4`
- `-100 <= nums[i] <= 100`
- `0 <= index < nums.length`
- `-100 <= val <= 100`
- `0 <= left <= right < nums.length`
- At most `3 * 10^4` calls will be made to `update` and `sumRange`.
constraints:
`1 <= nums.length <= 3 * 104`
`-100 <= nums[i] <= 100`
`0 <= index < nums.length`
`-100 <= val <= 100`
`0 <= left <= right < nums.length`
At most `3 * 104` calls will be made to `update` and `sumRange`.
hints: A prefix sum gives O(1) queries, but O(n) updates. A plain array gives O(1) updates, but O(n) queries. Can you find a middle ground that balances both operations?
Think about dividing the array into blocks. If you precompute the sum for each block, how would that change the cost of updates and queries?

A tree structure where each node stores the sum of a range of elements lets you handle both operations in O(log n). What if the root stores the total sum, its children store sums of the left and right halves, and so on recursively?
A Binary Indexed Tree (Fenwick Tree) achieves the same O(log n) bounds with less code. It uses the binary representation of indices to determine which ranges each position is responsible for.

title: Range Sum Query 2D - Mutable
description:
Given a 2D matrix `matrix`, handle multiple queries of the following types:
- **Update** the value of a cell in `matrix`.
- Calculate the **sum** of the elements of `matrix` inside the rectangle defined by its upper left corner `(row1, col1)` and lower right corner `(row2, col2)`.
Implement the `NumMatrix` class:
- `NumMatrix(int[][] matrix)` Initializes the object with the integer matrix `matrix`.
- `void update(int row, int col, int val)` Updates the value of `matrix[row][col]` to be `val`.
- `int sumRegion(int row1, int col1, int row2, int col2)` Returns the sum of the elements of `matrix` inside the rectangle defined by its upper left corner `(row1, col1)` and lower right corner `(row2, col2)`.
##### **Example 1:**
**Input:** `operations = ["NumMatrix", "sumRegion", "update", "sumRegion"]`, `arguments = [[[[3,0,1,4,2],[5,6,3,2,1],[1,2,0,1,5],[4,1,0,1,7],[1,0,3,0,5]]],[2,1,4,3],[3,2,2],[2,1,4,3]]`
**Output:** `[null, 8, null, 10]`
- `NumMatrix numMatrix = new NumMatrix([[3,0,1,4,2],[5,6,3,2,1],[1,2,0,1,5],[4,1,0,1,7],[1,0,3,0,5]]);`
- `numMatrix.sumRegion(2, 1, 4, 3);` // return 8
- `numMatrix.update(3, 2, 2);` // matrix[3][2] changes from 0 to 2
- `numMatrix.sumRegion(2, 1, 4, 3);` // return 10
##### **Constraints:**
- `m == matrix.length`
- `n == matrix[i].length`
- `1 <= m, n <= 200`
- `-10^4 <= matrix[i][j] <= 10^4`
- `0 <= row1 <= row2 < m`
- `0 <= col1 <= col2 < n`
- At most `5000` calls will be made to `sumRegion` and `update`.
- `-10^5 <= val <= 10^5`
constraints:
m == matrix.length
n == matrix[i].length
1 <= m, n <= 200
-10^4 <= matrix[i][j] <= 10^4
0 <= row1 <= row2 < m
0 <= col1 <= col2 < n
At most 5000 calls will be made to sumRegion and update.
-10^5 <= val <= 10^5
hints: Think about the immutable version first. A 2D prefix sum gives O(1) queries but breaks when the matrix changes. What if you only precomputed prefix sums along one dimension (rows) so that an update only invalidates one row?

If you maintain a prefix sum for each row independently, updating a cell means rebuilding only that row's prefix sum in O(n). A sumRegion query iterates over the relevant rows, using each row's prefix sum to get the column range in O(1). That's O(m) per query.
For something faster, think about Binary Indexed Trees (Fenwick Trees). A 1D BIT gives O(log n) point updates and prefix sum queries. Can you extend this to 2D?
A 2D BIT of size (m+1) x (n+1) supports both update and prefix sum query in O(log m * log n). For sumRegion, use inclusion-exclusion on four prefix sum queries.

title: Count of Smaller Numbers After Self
description:
Given an integer array `nums`, return an integer array `counts` where `counts[i]` is the number of smaller elements to the right of `nums[i]`.
##### **Example 1:**
**Explanation:**
- To the right of 5 there are 2 smaller elements (2 and 1).
- To the right of 2 there is only 1 smaller element (1).
- To the right of 6 there is 1 smaller element (1).
- To the right of 1 there is 0 smaller elements.
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Example 3:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= nums.length <= 10^5`
- `-10^4 <= nums[i] <= 10^4`
hints: Think about processing the array from right to left. As you move left, you've already "seen" all the elements to the right of the current position.
If you maintained a sorted structure of all elements you've seen so far (processing right to left), how would you count how many are smaller than the current element?
A Binary Indexed Tree (Fenwick Tree) can answer "how many elements have value less than X?" in O(log n) if you index by value. The value range is small enough (20,001) to make this practical.

Alternatively, think about merge sort. During the merge step, when an element from the left half is placed into the result, the number of already-placed elements from the right half tells you how many right-side elements were smaller.

title: Falling Squares
description:
There are several squares being dropped onto the X-axis of a 2D plane.
You are given a 2D integer array `positions` where `positions[i] = [left_i, sideLength_i]` represents the `i-th` square with a side length of `sideLength_i` that is dropped with its left edge aligned with X-coordinate `left_i`.
Each square is dropped one at a time from a height above any landed squares. It then falls downward (negative Y direction) until it either lands on the top side of another square or on the X-axis. A square brushing the left/right side of another square does not count as landing on it. Once it lands, it freezes in place and cannot be moved.
After each square is dropped, you must record the height of the current tallest stack of squares.
Return an integer array `ans` where `ans[i]` represents the height of the tallest stack of squares after dropping the `i-th` square.
##### **Example 1:**
**Explanation:**
- After the first drop, the tallest stack is square 1 with a height of 2.
- After the second drop, the tallest stack is squares 1 and 2 with a height of 2 + 3 = 5.
- After the third drop, the tallest stack is still 5 since square 3 lands on the X-axis at position 6 and doesn't overlap with anything.
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= positions.length <= 1000`
- `1 <= left_i <= 10^8`
- `1 <= sideLength_i <= 10^6`
constraints:
`1 <= positions.length <= 1000`
`1 <= lefti <= 108`
`1 <= sideLengthi <= 106`
hints: Each square occupies an interval [left, left + side). When a new square drops, you need to find the maximum height among all previously placed squares whose intervals overlap with this new interval.
Two intervals [a, b) and [c, d) overlap if and only if a < d and c < b. Use this to check which previous squares a new square lands on.

For the brute force, keep track of each placed square's interval and its top height. For each new square, scan all previous squares, find the max height in the overlapping region, and set the new square's top to that max plus its side length.
For a more scalable approach, think about coordinate compression. Map all left and right coordinates to a compact range, then use a segment tree to do range-max queries and range-update operations.

title: Block Placement Queries
description:
There exists an infinite number line, on which you can place obstacles.
You are given a 0-indexed 2D integer array `queries`, where each `queries[i]` is either `[1, x]` (type 1) or `[2, x, sz]` (type 2).
There are two types of queries:
- For a query of type 1, you are given `queries[i] = [1, x]`. Set an obstacle at position `x`. It is guaranteed that there is no obstacle at position `x` when this query is made.
- For a query of type 2, you are given `queries[i] = [2, x, sz]`. Check if it is possible to place a block of size `sz` anywhere in the range `[0, x]` on the number line, so that the block lies entirely within `[0, x]` and does not intersect any obstacle. The block may touch an obstacle at either end.
Note that an obstacle at position 0 already exists.
Return a boolean array `results` where `results[i]` is the result of the `i`-th query of type 2. For each type 2 query, the result is `true` if you can place the block, and `false` otherwise.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= queries.length <= 15000`
- `2 <= queries[i].length <= 3`
- `1 <= queries[i][0] <= 2`
- `1 <= queries[i][1] <= min(5 * 10^4, 3 * queries.length)`
- For type 2 queries: `1 <= queries[i][2] <= min(5 * 10^4, 3 * queries.length)`
constraints:
`1 <= queries.length <= 15 * 104`
`2 <= queries[i].length <= 3`
`1 <= queries[i][0] <= 2`
`1 <= x, sz <= min(5 * 104, 3 * queries.length)`
The input is generated such that for queries of type 1, no obstacle exists at distance `x` when the query is asked.
The input is generated such that there is at least one query of type 2.
hints: When an obstacle is placed, it splits an existing gap into two smaller gaps. Think about how to efficiently track these gaps as obstacles are added.

For a type 2 query with position x, you need the maximum gap between consecutive obstacles in [0, x]. A sorted set of obstacles lets you find gaps, but how do you get the maximum gap in a prefix efficiently?
Consider using a segment tree where each position stores the gap to the previous obstacle, and query for the max in a range. When an obstacle is added, only two gap values change.

Use a segment tree indexed by obstacle positions that stores gap sizes. When an obstacle is added at position p, find its neighbors in a sorted set, remove the old gap, and add two new gaps. For type 2 queries, query the segment tree for the maximum gap value in the range of obstacles within [0, x].

title: Longest Balanced Subarray II
description:
You are given an integer array `nums`.
A subarray is called balanced if the number of distinct even numbers in the subarray is equal to the number of distinct odd numbers.
Return the length of the longest balanced subarray.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Example 3:**
##### **Constraints:**
- **1 <= nums.length <= 10****5**
- **1 <= nums[i] <= 10****5**
constraints:
1 <= nums.length <= 10^5
nums[i] is either 0 or 1
0 <= k <= nums.length
hints: A value only changes the distinct balance when its latest occurrence moves.
Treat every odd value as +1 and every even value as -1.
When a value repeats, remove its old suffix contribution and add its new suffix contribution.
To maximize the subarray length, find the earliest prior position with the same transformed balance.

title: Number of Pairs Satisfying Inequality
description:
You are given two **0-indexed** integer arrays `nums1` and `nums2`, each of size `n`, and an integer `diff`. Find the number of **pairs** `(i, j)` such that:
- `0 <= i < j <= n - 1`, and
- `nums1[i] - nums1[j] <= nums2[i] - nums2[j] + diff`
Return the **number of pairs** that satisfy the conditions.
##### **Example 1:**
**Input:** `nums1 = [3,2,5]`, `nums2 = [2,2,1]`, `diff = 1`
**Output:** `3`
**Explanation:** There are 3 pairs that satisfy the conditions: (0, 1) since `3 - 2 <= 2 - 2 + 1`, (0, 2) since `3 - 5 <= 2 - 1 + 1`, and (1, 2) since `2 - 5 <= 2 - 1 + 1`.
##### **Example 2:**
**Input:** `nums1 = [3,-1]`, `nums2 = [-2,2]`, `diff = -1`
**Output:** `0`
**Explanation:** Since there are no pairs that satisfy the conditions, we return 0.
##### **Constraints:**
- `n == nums1.length == nums2.length`
- `2 <= n <= 10^5`
- `-10^4 <= nums1[i], nums2[i] <= 10^4`
- `-10^4 <= diff <= 10^4`
constraints:
n == nums1.length == nums2.length
2 <= n <= 10^5
-10^4 <= nums1[i], nums2[i] <= 10^4
-10^4 <= diff <= 10^4
hints: Simplify the inequality first. Rearrange terms so that all references to index i are on one side and all references to index j are on the other. Define a helper array to make the condition cleaner.

After simplification, you need to count pairs (i, j) with i < j where arr[i] <= arr[j] + diff. This is similar to counting inversions, but with a shifted threshold instead of a strict less-than comparison.

Merge sort preserves the relative ordering constraint (i < j) across the two halves: every left-half element has a smaller index than every right-half element. During the merge step, both halves are sorted, so for each element in the right half, a forward-moving pointer counts how many left-half elements satisfy the condition.

Alternatively, you can use a Binary Indexed Tree (BIT). Process elements left to right. For each index j, query the BIT for how many previously inserted values arr[i] satisfy arr[i] <= arr[j] + diff, then insert arr[j] into the BIT.

title: Employee Free Time
description:
We are given a list `schedule` of employees, which represents the working time for each employee.
Each employee has a list of non-overlapping `Intervals`, and these intervals are in sorted order.
Return the list of finite intervals representing **common, positive-length free time** for *all* employees, also in sorted order.
(Even though we are representing `Intervals` in the form `[x, y]`, the objects inside are `Intervals`, not lists or arrays. For example, `schedule[0][0].start = 1`, `schedule[0][0].end = 2`, and `schedule[0][0][0]` is not defined.)
Also, we wouldn't include intervals like `[5, 5]` in our answer, as they have zero length.
##### **Example 1:**
*There are a total of three employees, and all common free time intervals would be [3,4].*
##### **Example 2:**
*There are a total of three employees, and all common free time intervals would be [5,6] and [7,9].*
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= schedule.length, schedule[i].length <= 50`
- `0 <= schedule[i][j].start < schedule[i][j].end <= 10^8`
- Each employee's intervals are non-overlapping and sorted by start time.
constraints:
1 <= schedule.length, schedule[i].length <= 50
0 <= schedule[i][j].start < schedule[i][j].end <= 10^8
Each employee's intervals are non-overlapping and sorted by start time.
hints: You don't need to track which employee an interval belongs to. Try flattening all intervals into a single list.
Once you have all intervals together, think about merging overlapping intervals. The gaps between merged intervals are the free time.
Since each employee's intervals are already sorted, you can use a min-heap to efficiently merge them in order without sorting the entire list. Pull the interval with the smallest start time first.
After merging, iterate through the merged intervals. Whenever the start of the current interval is strictly greater than the end of the previous one, that gap is a free time interval.

title: Minimum Interval to Include Each Query
description:
You are given a 2D integer array `intervals`, where `intervals[i] = [left_i, right_i]` describes the `i`th interval starting at `left_i` and ending at `right_i` (inclusive). The size of an interval is defined as the number of integers it contains, or more formally `right_i - left_i + 1`.
You are also given an integer array `queries`. The answer to the `j`th query is the size of the smallest interval `i` such that `left_i <= queries[j] <= right_i`. If no such interval exists, the answer is `-1`.
Return an array containing the answers to the queries.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= intervals.length <= 10^5`
- `1 <= queries.length <= 10^5`
- `intervals[i].length == 2`
- `1 <= left_i <= right_i <= 10^7`
- `1 <= queries[j] <= 10^7`
constraints:
`1 <= intervals.length <= 105`
`1 <= queries.length <= 105`
`intervals[i].length == 2`
`1 <= lefti <= righti <= 107`
`1 <= queries[j] <= 107`
hints: If you sorted both the intervals and the queries, could you avoid re-checking intervals you've already processed?
As you process queries from smallest to largest, intervals that start before or at the query point become active. But some active intervals might have already ended before the query point.
You need a data structure that lets you efficiently find the smallest active interval and remove intervals that no longer cover the current query. A min-heap keyed by interval size works well here.

Sort intervals by left endpoint, sort queries (keeping original indices). Sweep left to right: for each query, push all intervals that start at or before the query, pop any that end before it, and the heap top gives the smallest valid interval.

title: The Skyline Problem
description:
A city's **skyline** is the outer contour of the silhouette formed by all the buildings in that city when viewed from a distance. Given the locations and heights of all the buildings, return the skyline formed by these buildings collectively.
The geometric information of each building is given in the array `buildings` where `buildings[i] = [left_i, right_i, height_i]`:
- `left_i` is the x coordinate of the left edge of the `i-th` building.
- `right_i` is the x coordinate of the right edge of the `i-th` building.
- `height_i` is the height of the `i-th` building.
You may assume all buildings are perfect rectangles grounded on an absolutely flat surface at height 0.
The **skyline** should be represented as a list of "key points" **sorted by their x-coordinate** in the form `[[x1, y1], [x2, y2], ...]`. Each key point is the left endpoint of some horizontal segment in the skyline except the last point in the list, which always has a y-coordinate 0 and is used to mark the skyline's termination where the rightmost building ends. Any ground between two adjacent buildings should be part of the skyline's contour as well.
**Note:** There must be no consecutive horizontal lines of equal height in the output skyline. For instance, `[..., [2, 3], [4, 5], [7, 5], [11, 5], [12, 7], ...]` is not acceptable; the three lines of height 5 should be merged into one in the final output as such: `[..., [2, 3], [4, 5], [12, 7], ...]`.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= buildings.length <= 10^4`
- `0 <= left_i < right_i <= 2^31 - 1`
- `1 <= height_i <= 2^31 - 1`
- `buildings` is sorted by `left_i` in non-decreasing order.
constraints:
`1 <= buildings.length <= 104`
`0 <= lefti < righti <= 231 - 1`
`1 <= heighti <= 231 - 1`
`buildings` is sorted by `lefti` in non-decreasing order.
hints: Think about the x-coordinates where the skyline height can change. It can only change where a building starts or where a building ends.

At each such critical x-coordinate, you need to know the maximum height of all buildings that are currently active (started but haven't ended yet). What data structure gives you fast access to the maximum?
Use a max-heap (or a sorted multiset) to track active building heights. At each event, add or remove heights, then check if the max height changed. If it did, that's a new key point in the skyline.
Be careful about the order of processing when multiple events share the same x-coordinate. If a building starts and another ends at the same x, process the start first (to avoid a false dip to zero).

title: Rectangle Area II
description:
You are given a 2D array of axis-aligned `rectangles`. For each `rectangle[i] = [x1, y1, x2, y2]`, it represents a rectangle with bottom-left corner `(x1, y1)` and top-right corner `(x2, y2)`.
Return the total area covered by all rectangles in the plane. Since the answer may be too large, return it modulo `10^9 + 7`.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= rectangles.length <= 200`
- `rectangles[i].length == 4`
- `0 <= x1 < x2 <= 10^9`
- `0 <= y1 < y2 <= 10^9`
constraints:
`1 <= rectangles.length <= 200`
`rectanges[i].length == 4`
`0 <= xi1, yi1, xi2, yi2 <= 109`
`xi1 <= xi2`
`yi1 <= yi2`
All rectangles have non zero area.
hints: The coordinates can be up to 10^9, but there are at most 200 rectangles. How many unique x-coordinates and y-coordinates can there be? Can you map them to a smaller range?

Coordinate compression: collect all unique x and y values, sort them, and think of the plane as divided into small cells by these coordinates. Each cell is either fully covered or not covered by any rectangle.

After compressing coordinates, you can check each cell in the compressed grid. For each cell, check if any rectangle covers it. If so, add the cell's actual area (using original coordinates) to the total.

For a faster approach, sweep a vertical line from left to right. At each x-coordinate, maintain which y-intervals are currently active. The area contribution is the active y-length times the distance to the next x-coordinate.

title: Perfect Rectangle
description:
Given an array `rectangles` where `rectangles[i] = [xi, yi, ai, bi]` represents an axis-aligned rectangle. The bottom-left point of the rectangle is `(xi, yi)` and the top-right point of it is `(ai, bi)`.
Return `true` if all the rectangles together form an exact cover of a rectangular region.
##### **Example 1:**
##### **Example 2:**
##### **Example 3:**
##### **Constraints:**
- `1 <= rectangles.length <= 2 * 10^4`
- `rectangles[i].length == 4`
- `-10^5 <= xi, yi, ai, bi <= 10^5`
constraints:
1 <= rectangles.length <= 2 * 10^4
rectangles[i].length == 4
-10^5 <= a_i < c_i <= 10^5
-10^5 <= b_i < d_i <= 10^5
hints: Think about what properties a perfect rectangular cover must have. What must be true about the total area?
The area check is necessary but not sufficient. Consider the corners of each small rectangle. In a perfect tiling, how many times should each corner point appear?

Every internal corner point (not on the boundary of the big rectangle) must appear an even number of times (2 or 4). Only the four corners of the bounding rectangle should appear an odd number of times (exactly once each). Use a set toggle to track this.

Combine two checks: (1) the sum of all small rectangle areas equals the bounding rectangle area, and (2) after toggling all corner points in a set, exactly 4 points remain, and they are the corners of the bounding rectangle.
